quotes.quote: Even Bing, widely considered a failure, on last reported revenue and current P/E ratio, would be 12th most valuable tech company in the world, between Tencent and ASML.
quotes.summary: The dominant narrative that Steve Ballmer was a failed CEO who held Microsoft back until Satya Nadella saved the company is fundamentally wrong. While Ballmer faced intense criticism for missing mobile and other trends, he actually oversaw massive revenue growth (from $14-22B to $83B), made crucial long-term bets that define Microsoft today (Azure, Office 365, Bing, Xbox), and built the enterprise sales machine that powers the company. He inherited a company hamstrung by antitrust constraints and brutal internal politics, then cleared both obstacles while subsidizing multi-billion dollar moonshots that critics derided but which positioned Microsoft for decades of success.
quotes.key_insight: Ballmer's real achievement was making massive, heavily-criticized long-term bets that set up trillion-dollar business units and clearing political obstacles so thoroughly that his successor's success made him look incompetent by comparison.

quotes.quote: This very silly bot that doesn't guess until it can guess everything is much stronger than most Codenames players.
quotes.summary: This post explores how a Codenames player could dominate the game by memorizing the 40 setup cards that come with the game, rather than relying on word associations. The author built a simple bot that uses only positional information from these cards and found it beats most human players, demonstrating that the game has an exploitable side-channel vulnerability. Despite this flaw, the author argues Codenames still has better replayability than most word games, which typically break down after just a few plays when players memorize common word associations.
quotes.key_insight: Even seemingly simple games can have exploitable side-channels that reveal the gap between designed and actual gameplay, much like how AI tools are already 'good enough' for many software tasks despite not being anywhere near perfect.

quotes.quote: The relevant question isn't 'when will AI allow laypeople to create better software than programmers?' but 'when will AI allow laypeople to create software that's as good as phone trees and crappy voice recognition are for customer support?'

quotes.quote: AI apologists insist this is fine when it comes to things like changing someone's race or ethnicity...it would be absurd to argue that it's fine for my tire change appointment to have been converted to an oil change appointment because other companies have schedulers that convert oil change appointments to tire change appointments
quotes.tone: analytical and methodical with underlying frustration
quotes.summary: Luu examines how discussions about AI/ML bias reveal a double standard compared to 'classical' software bugs, where people frequently deny that outputs opposite to user intent (like turning Asian faces white in professional photos) constitute bugs at all. He argues that encoding biases into automated systems isn't new to ML—it's been happening throughout computing history—but ML has made these biases more legible to lay people. The fundamental problem is that consumer markets reward velocity over quality, making it structurally difficult to address bias even when technical solutions might exist.
quotes.key_insight: AI bias isn't a new problem but rather the latest manifestation of how automated systems have always encoded societal biases—the real challenge is that market incentives systematically favor shipping fast over fixing these issues, making proposed solutions based on 'caring more' or 'diverse hiring' structurally doomed to fail without addressing the underlying economic forces.

quotes.quote: My feeling is that the 'natural', as in lowest energy and most straightforward state for institutions and products is that they don't work very well. quotes.quote: In order for the BE memo to seem credible, the reader must have large and significant gaps in their understanding of the tech industry.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu analyzes leaked internal FTC memos from the 2011-2012 Google antitrust investigation, revealing how the Bureau of Economics (BE) made fundamental errors about the tech industry that contradicted the Bureau of Competition's (BC) case. BE staff argued mobile was unimportant, switching costs were trivial, and Google's anticompetitive practices had minimal impact—positions that were demonstrably wrong at the time and proven incorrect by subsequent events. The investigation was dropped despite BC making a defensible case, highlighting a systemic problem: tech policy decisions are made by people who lack basic understanding of the industry they're regulating.
quotes.key_insight: Effective tech regulation requires practitioners with hands-on industry expertise who understand how these systems actually work, not just economists and lawyers applying abstract models that contradict observable market behavior and incentives.

quotes.quote: When the authors of the BE memo make a prediction, they seem to have a facility for predicting the opposite of what will happen.

quotes.quote: In the case of Discourse, a hardware engineer is an embarrassment not deserving of a job if they can't hit 90% of the performance of an all-time-great performance team but, as a software engineer, delivering 3% the performance of a non-highly-optimized application like MyBB is no problem.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu demonstrates that while web bloat was previously constrained by slow internet connections, CPU performance for web apps hasn't scaled as quickly as bandwidth, making modern websites increasingly inaccessible to users with low-end devices even with fast internet. Testing popular platforms on devices like the Tecno Spark 8C and Itel P32 reveals that newer platforms (Discourse, Medium, Substack) can be 20-30x slower than older alternatives (MyBB, old WordPress), with many modern sites being completely unusable or crashing browsers on budget devices. The article challenges the widespread developer attitude that CPU speed is "infinite" and that only wealthy users with premium devices matter, noting that globally accessible design remains achievable as demonstrated by older, simpler platforms.
quotes.key_insight: Developer assumptions that CPU speed is infinite and that only wealthy users matter have created a web where modern platforms can be orders of magnitude slower than decade-old alternatives, making much of the internet unusable for billions of people with low-end devices despite these devices being powerful enough to run 3D games and compile complex codebases.

quotes.quote: It's fairly common for programmers to expect that hardware will solve all their problems, and then, when that doesn't happen, pass the issue onto the user, explaining why the programmer needn't do anything to help the user.

quotes.quote: 36902 people expressed an opinion on what constitutes a vehicle in the park and they came up with 9432 distinct opinions, for an average of ~3.9 people, per distinct expressed opinion.
quotes.summary: Using a simple thought experiment about what vehicles should be allowed in a park, this post demonstrates that it's impossible to get people to agree on moderation policies even in trivial cases. Analysis of survey data reveals that even among homogeneous groups like Hacker News readers, only 11.7% agreed on the 'obvious' answer, with 9,432 distinct opinions expressed by 36,902 respondents. The problem becomes exponentially worse with emotionally-charged issues, where opinion spaces are 'fractally contentious' - people with nearly identical views will fight bitterly over minute differences, making platform-wide moderation policies that satisfy users fundamentally impossible at scale.
quotes.key_insight: People dramatically underestimate how much disagreement exists even on seemingly simple rules, and this disagreement becomes fractally worse with emotionally-charged topics, making universal agreement on platform moderation policies mathematically impossible at scale.

quotes.quote: Many (and probably actually most) people are overconfident when predicting what other people think is obvious and often incorrectly assume that other people will think the same thoughts and find the same things obvious.

quotes.quote: I don't think it's controversial to say that in general, a lot of things get worse as platforms get bigger. For example, when I ran a Twitter poll to see what people I'm loosely connected to think, only 2.6% thought that huge company platforms have the best moderation and spam/fraud filtering.
quotes.summary: Luu systematically dismantles the claim that only large tech companies can effectively handle moderation, spam filtering, and fraud detection. Through extensive empirical evidence, he demonstrates that larger platforms consistently perform worse than smaller ones in these areas, despite having vastly more resources. He argues this stems from three core issues: 1) larger platforms attract more sophisticated attacks that overwhelm even advanced ML systems, 2) support quality degrades as companies scale up and optimize for profit over user experience, and 3) scaling makes consensus on content policies impossible. The post includes hundreds of documented examples of failures at companies like Meta, Google, Amazon, and others, showing that theoretical economies of scale don't translate to better outcomes when companies choose not to deploy adequate resources.
quotes.key_insight: The problem with moderation, spam, and support at large tech companies isn't a technical limitation or lack of resources, but a deliberate business choice to prioritize growth and profit over user safety, compounded by organizational complexity that makes systems opaque even to employees working on them.

quotes.quote: If Meta had a version of the internal vision statement of a power company a friend mine worked for ('Reliable energy, at low cost, for generations.') and operated like that power company did, trying to create a good experience for the user instead of maximizing profit plus creating the metaverse, they could've spent the $50B they spent on the metaverse on moderation.

The post includes hundreds of documented examples of failures at companies like Meta, Google, Amazon, and others, showing that theoretical economies of scale don't translate to better outcomes when companies choose not to deploy adequate resources.
quotes.key_insight: The problem with moderation, spam, and support at large tech companies isn't a technical limitation or lack of resources, but a deliberate business choice to prioritize growth and profit over user safety, compounded by organizational complexity that makes systems opaque even to employees working on them.

quotes.quote: If I were ten years younger and started doing something online in 2023 instead of 2013, more likely than not, I would've tried streaming before I tried blogging.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu examines why people post content on platforms like Twitter/Mastodon ("bad platforms") instead of traditional blogs, despite frequent complaints from readers. He identifies four main reasons: higher engagement (short-form platforms vastly dominate long-form in usage and valuation), community presence (people post where their friends are), lower emotional friction (many creators find it mentally easier to write in short chunks than complete blog posts), and better monetization (especially for video, which generates far more revenue than text). While Luu personally prefers long-form text content, he recognizes that much of the content people complain about wouldn't exist at all if creators were forced to use "better" platforms.
quotes.key_insight: Complaints that content should be on a "better" platform fundamentally misunderstand that platform constraints enable rather than prevent most online content creation, and the content wouldn't exist at all if forced into the critic's preferred format.

quotes.quote: As we previously discussed, it's common for summaries to be wildly wrong, even when they're summarizing a short paper that's easily read by laypeople, so of course summaries of a 200-page report are likely to be misleading at best.
quotes.summary: This post analyzes the Quinn Emanuel report on Cruise's handling of a 2023 pedestrian accident where their autonomous vehicle dragged a pedestrian 20 feet after the initial collision. Luu argues that while media coverage portrayed Cruise as either transparently cooperative or deliberately deceptive, the actual 200-page report reveals both better and worse behavior than reported. The core issue wasn't intentional deception but catastrophic leadership failures, poor regulatory understanding, and an adversarial mindset toward regulators—behavior that, ironically, would be considered normal or even above-board at most tech companies, highlighting a dangerous culture clash between "move fast" tech culture and safety-critical systems.
quotes.key_insight: The Cruise incident reveals a fundamental incompatibility between standard tech company culture—where passive disclosure and assuming others will ask questions is normal—and the proactive transparency requirements of safety-critical systems, demonstrating that tech companies entering regulated industries need cultural transformation, not just compliance checklists.

quotes.quote: In their original Page Rank paper, Sergei Brin and Larry Page noted that ad-based search is inherently not incentive aligned with providing good results: 'we expect that advertising funded search engines will be inherently biased towards the advertisers and away from the needs of the Consumers.'
quotes.summary: Through systematic testing of naive queries across Google, Bing, Kagi, Marginalia, Mwmbl, and ChatGPT, this post empirically demonstrates that major search engines now return significantly worse results than a decade ago, dominated by SEO spam, scams, and deliberately fraudulent content. Marginalia, a single-person search engine, often outperforms trillion-dollar companies by returning fewer scams despite having less comprehensive coverage. The findings validate complaints that search quality has degraded substantially, contradicting defenders who claim concerns are overblown.
quotes.key_insight: The degradation of search quality is not subjective complaining but empirically demonstrable through simple queries, revealing how profit incentives have corrupted what was once Google's core competency while demonstrating that small, independent alternatives can outperform billion-dollar companies when properly incentivized.

quotes.quote: Fundamentally, the thing that derailed so many of Kurzweil's predictions is that he relied on the idea of exponential and accelerating growth in basically every area he can imagine
quotes.summary: This post systematically evaluates the prediction accuracy of well-known futurists (Kurzweil, Kaku, Toffler, etc.) by grading their concrete predictions, finding accuracy rates of 3-10% for most futurists. The author contrasts these poor results with more accurate predictors who had deep domain expertise (like Steve Yegge and Bill Gates) and identifies common failure modes: cocktail-party level understanding, blindly extrapolating exponential trends, panacea thinking, and failure to learn from past errors. The key finding is that accurate prediction requires genuine domain expertise and probabilistic thinking, not broad theories or 'big ideas.'
quotes.key_insight: Accurate futurism requires deep domain expertise combined with rigorous self-criticism rather than broad theoretical frameworks - most celebrated futurists fail because they apply cocktail-party level understanding with unjustified certainty about exponential trends while never seriously evaluating their past errors.

quotes.quote: Wave is a $1.7B company with 70 engineers whose product is a CRUD app that adds and subtracts numbers.
quotes.summary: Wave, a $1.7B fintech company with 70 engineers, deliberately uses a simple Python monolith architecture instead of trendy microservices. The author argues that for most applications, even at massive scale, simple architectures are more cost-effective and allow engineers to focus on user value rather than managing architectural complexity. Despite the tech industry's obsession with sophisticated architectures, companies like Wave and Stack Overflow have scaled to billions in value using boring, monolithic approaches.
quotes.key_insight: Simple architectures remain undervalued in an industry that fetishizes complexity, even though they enable most companies to scale profitably while keeping engineering focused on delivering user value rather than managing self-imposed architectural overhead.

quotes.quote: Whatever abstract arguments people make, inefficiencies persisted for decades.
quotes.summary: This post argues that market efficiency assumptions are often wrong in practice - both for products and services. Through numerous examples (from hiring to data infrastructure to shipping to car safety), Luu demonstrates that companies frequently fail to produce working products, informed consumers struggle to identify quality, and 'buy vs build' decisions are often driven by cultural assumptions rather than rational analysis. The persistence of inefficiency occurs because there's weak selection pressure for quality when buyers can't distinguish good products from bad ones, and because cultural expectations (like 'focus on core competencies') override technical reasoning.
quotes.key_insight: Markets fail to enforce efficiency when information asymmetry prevents buyers from distinguishing quality, allowing broken products to persist and thrive through marketing while cultural assumptions override rational decision-making at both organizational and individual levels.

quotes.quote: There's something a bit odd about height seeming more correlated to success as a consultant or a programmer than in baseball, where height directly conveys an advantage.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that talent identification in tech (and other fields) is deeply flawed, relying on irrelevant surface characteristics like height, confidence, and social class markers rather than actual performance. Drawing parallels to baseball's decades-long resistance to data-driven scouting despite measurable performance metrics, he demonstrates that tech companies perform even worse at talent assessment since individual performance is much harder to measure. Through examples ranging from baseball scouts to university admissions to corporate promotions, he shows how gatekeepers consistently pattern-match on appearance and background rather than capability, creating systemic bias that filters out talented people who don't "look the part." Despite this pessimistic systemic view, he argues individuals and small teams can gain significant advantages by ignoring these irrelevant criteria and focusing on actual output.
quotes.key_insight: Tech industry talent assessment is systematically biased toward irrelevant surface characteristics (height, confidence, social class) rather than actual performance, creating both massive inefficiency and opportunity for those willing to ignore conventional proxies and focus on demonstrated output.

quotes.quote: I suspect this is not so different from the CMU professor who admitted a promising student on the spot, it just happens that a lot of people pattern match 'smart teenage boy with a story about why their grades and SAT scores are bad' to 'promising potential prodigy' and 'girl from rural Canada with the top grade in her high school class who hasn't really used a computer before and dresses like a poor person from rural Canada because she's paying for college while raising her younger brother because their parents basically abandoned both of them' to 'homeless person who doesn't belong in engineering'.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that talent identification in tech (and other fields) is deeply flawed, relying on irrelevant surface characteristics like height, confidence, and social class markers rather than actual performance. Drawing parallels to baseball's decades-long resistance to data-driven scouting despite measurable performance metrics, he demonstrates that tech companies perform even worse at talent assessment since individual performance is much harder to measure. Through examples ranging from baseball scouts to university admissions to corporate promotions, he shows how gatekeepers consistently pattern-match on appearance and background rather than capability, creating systemic bias that filters out talented people who don't "look the part." Despite this pessimistic systemic view, he argues individuals and small teams can gain significant advantages by ignoring these irrelevant criteria and focusing on actual output.
quotes.key_insight: Tech industry talent assessment is systematically biased toward irrelevant surface characteristics (height, confidence, social class) rather than actual performance, creating both massive inefficiency and opportunity for those willing to ignore conventional proxies and focus on demonstrated output.

quotes.quote: People who have a superficial understanding of topic assume that the topic is as complex as their understanding of the topic instead of realizing that only knowing a bit about a topic means that they're missing an understanding of the full complexity of a topic.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that people with superficial knowledge of complex fields routinely propose simplistic solutions to hard problems, failing to recognize the hidden complexity they don't understand. He demonstrates this with examples from civil engineering, computer architecture, and physics, showing how fields contain orders of magnitude more complexity than outsiders (or even insiders taking a high-level view) typically assume. The root cause is people assuming a topic's full complexity matches their limited understanding of it, rather than recognizing their knowledge gaps.
quotes.key_insight: The Dunning-Kruger effect manifests not just as overconfidence in one's abilities, but as a failure to recognize that fields contain hidden layers of complexity that become visible only through deep engagement—expertise reveals not just answers but previously invisible questions.

quotes.quote: It's easy to come up with cocktail party solutions to problems by not understanding the problem, assuming the problem is artificially simple, and then coming up with a solution to the imagined problem.

quotes.quote: Every single incident so far has at least mentioned cache. In fact, for a long time, cache was probably the #1 source of bringing the site down for a while. In my first six months, every time I restarted a cache server, it was a SEV-0 by today's standards.
quotes.summary: This post documents a decade of severe cache-related incidents at Twitter (2012-2022), revealing that cache was historically the #1 source of site outages. The incidents demonstrate how cache failures cascade through distributed systems due to positive feedback loops, client misbehavior, and infrastructure issues. Most major outages weren't caused by cache logic bugs, but rather by poorly mitigated interactions between cache and surrounding infrastructure—kernel networking issues, hardware problems, aggressive client retry policies, and resource exhaustion under load.
quotes.key_insight: Most distributed systems failures in production aren't caused by logic bugs but by insufficiently mitigated positive feedback loops triggered by performance anomalies in the infrastructure stack, compounded by institutional knowledge loss that causes teams to repeatedly encounter similar failure modes.

quotes.quote: There's a kind of Gresham's law of stories in play, where incorrect stories tend to win out over correct stories.

quotes.quote: Cache failure modes are also interesting because, when cache is used to serve a significant fraction of requests or fraction of data, cache outages or even degradation can easily cause a total outage because an architecture designed with cache performance in mind will not (and should not) have backing DB store performance that's sufficient to keep the site up.

quotes.quote: Copying what's worked for someone else is unlikely to work for you, making 'write how I write' bad advice.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that writing advice framed as "how to write" is typically just "write how I write" in disguise, which fails because successful writing styles vary enormously across dimensions like length, tone, detail level, and structure. He demonstrates this by comparing Joel Spolsky, Paul Graham, Steve Yegge, and Julia Evans—all highly successful but with fundamentally different approaches. Rather than copying others' superficial techniques (which usually backfires), writers should start from their own goals and aptitudes, then develop processes and styles that serve those goals.
quotes.key_insight: Effective writing emerges from aligning your process and style with your specific goals and aptitudes rather than copying successful writers' superficial techniques, which inevitably fails because it misses the underlying principles that make their approach work for them.

quotes.quote: If we look at 99%-ile latency, we can see that it's ~16ms when measured at the server and ~240ms when measured at the client, a factor of 15 difference.
quotes.summary: This post examines systematic measurement problems in Twitter's latency monitoring infrastructure that cause service owners to drastically underestimate real user-experienced latency. Luu identifies three critical issues: opaque latency hidden in uninstrumented parts of the stack (netty, kernel) that can be 15x higher than reported metrics, lack of proper cluster-wide aggregation leading to misleading averages of per-shard tail latencies, and minutely-resolution metrics that miss sub-second latency spikes. These measurement gaps force Twitter to overprovision servers and maintain stricter-than-necessary SLOs to compensate for the uncertainty, directly increasing operating costs.
quotes.key_insight: Common latency measurement practices—sampling at the wrong layer, averaging tail metrics, and low-resolution sampling—can underestimate real latency by orders of magnitude, forcing expensive overprovisioning to compensate for fundamentally broken observability.

quotes.quote: Taking the average of per-shard tail latency defeats the purpose of monitoring tail latency.

quotes.quote: Because load isn't, in general, evenly balanced across shards and the shard-level degradation in performance is so severe when we exceed 50% CPU utilization, this makes the practical limit much lower than 50% even during peak load events.
quotes.summary: Twitter's CPU-bound services were failing at around 50% container CPU utilization due to the Linux CFS scheduler's throttling mechanism. When services with large thread pools exceeded their reserved cores, the scheduler would let them burst above quota then throttle them to maintain average limits, causing severe tail latency degradation and forcing massive overprovisioning. Solutions included better thread pool defaults, the Finagle Offload Filter for separating IO and application work, and a kernel patch preventing containers from exceeding quotas instantaneously rather than on an amortized basis, yielding 15-60% cost reductions and eliminating throttling death spirals.
quotes.key_insight: Container CPU throttling based on amortized quotas creates a fundamental mismatch between how developers reason about parallelism and how the scheduler enforces limits, resulting in catastrophic underutilization and metastable failure modes that can only be fixed by preventing instantaneous quota violations rather than amortized ones.

quotes.quote: A subsecond stop-the-world GC pause could take many seconds of wallclock time to complete.

quotes.quote: What happens is that, when a service is under high load, it will throttle. Throttling doesn't prevent requests from coming into the shard that's throttled, so when the shard wakes up from being throttled, it has even more work to do than it had before it throttled, causing it to use even more CPU and throttle more quickly.

quotes.quote: I've never worked in an organization where this actually worked, where this actually enabled teams to effectively execute with dependencies on other teams.
quotes.summary: Organizations systematically fail by treating individuals as fungible units rather than recognizing that individual effectiveness varies by orders of magnitude. This manifests in roadmap planning where teams privately know projects will fail based on who's assigned, in HR systems that force headcount-based budgeting preventing hiring of exceptional talent, and in attrition management where 'normalizing' turnover disproportionately drives out the most effective employees. The cost of this legibility-driven approach is enormous in heavy-tailed distributions of talent, where the right individual can single-handedly increase a giant company's revenue by 0.7% or solve decades-old problems in foreign aid work.
quotes.key_insight: Treating individuals as interchangeable for organizational legibility and scalability systematically destroys value in systems with heavy-tailed talent distributions, where single exceptional individuals can create orders of magnitude more impact than average performers.

quotes.quote: People, as a whole, cannot be treated as an abstraction where the actions company leadership takes impacts everyone in the same way. The people who are most effective will be disproportionately likely to leave if you turn a knob that leads to increased attrition.

quotes.quote: Almost none of us are operating at anywhere near our capacity and what we think we can achieve is often a major limiting factor.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that while process and incentives are important management tools, company culture has a measurably large impact on outcomes like productivity, quality, and individual skill development. He draws on his experience at Centaur, a high-velocity CPU company with minimal process but exceptional results, to show how cultural values around ownership, debugging rigor, and caring about quality can produce dramatically better outcomes than heavyweight processes at big tech companies. Culture is transmitted both to employees (who absorb the values of their environment) and carried forward when they leave, making it an underrated factor in career choices.
quotes.key_insight: Culture shapes what people believe is possible and what they care about, producing measurable differences in velocity and quality that often dwarf the impact of formal processes and incentive structures.

quotes.quote: You can't shake a stick without hitting a problem that's worth more to the company than my expected lifetime earnings and it's easy to knock off multiple such problems per year.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that while process and incentives are important management tools, company culture has a measurably large impact on outcomes like productivity, quality, and individual skill development. He draws on his experience at Centaur, a high-velocity CPU company with minimal process but exceptional results, to show how cultural values around ownership, debugging rigor, and caring about quality can produce dramatically better outcomes than heavyweight processes at big tech companies. Culture is transmitted both to employees (who absorb the values of their environment) and carried forward when they leave, making it an underrated factor in career choices.
quotes.key_insight: Culture shapes what people believe is possible and what they care about, producing measurable differences in velocity and quality that often dwarf the impact of formal processes and incentive structures.

quotes.summary: Dan Luu catalogs significant errors from his blog posts between 2013-2024, classifying them into categories like insufficient thought, naivete, insufficient verification, blunders, and confusing explanations. He draws a subjective line between 'serious' errors that materially affected his arguments versus minor typos, noting that many major errors came from his first three months of blogging. The post exemplifies his commitment to transparency and intellectual honesty, treating corrections as an ongoing process rather than a one-time exercise.
quotes.key_insight: Maintaining a public corrections log demonstrates intellectual honesty while revealing how many errors stem not from lack of knowledge but from rushing under time pressure or failing to verify when uncertainty exists.

quotes.quote: I find this a bit funny since I'm not a naturally quick programmer. Learning to program was a real struggle for me and I was pretty slow at it for a long time. My 'one weird trick' is that I've explicitly worked on speeding up things that I do frequently and most people have not.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that improving productivity and velocity is valuable despite common objections that working on the right thing matters more than speed, or that specific tasks don't consume enough time to optimize. He demonstrates through concrete examples how faster execution creates compound benefits: it enables more experimentation to find the right problems, allows long-shot ideas to become feasible, and creates phase transitions where entire workflows change. Contrary to the moral arguments against productivity, Luu shows that being more productive actually enables more leisure time and personal fulfillment.
quotes.key_insight: Velocity improvements create non-linear returns by enabling phase transitions that fundamentally change what work is feasible—turning multi-quarter team projects into day-long experiments and making long-shot ideas economically viable to pursue.

quotes.quote: It is commonly accepted, verging on a cliche, that you have no idea where your program spends time until you actually profile it, but the corollary that you also don't know where you spend your time until you've measured it is not nearly as accepted.

quotes.quote: If I try to solve some a problem by doing what everyone else is doing and go looking for problems where everyone else is looking, if I want to do something valuable, I'll have to do better than a lot of people, maybe even better than everybody else if the problem is really hard.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that willingness to look stupid is a significant advantage in learning and professional development. By not filtering thoughts or questions based on social acceptability, he's able to explore unconventional ideas that others dismiss, ask clarifying questions that deepen understanding, and pursue high-ROI opportunities that seem too foolish for others to attempt. While this approach causes regular social friction—from condescending interviewers to dismissive medical professionals—the compounding benefits of faster learning and access to overlooked opportunities far outweigh the costs of damaged reputation in individual interactions.
quotes.key_insight: Willingness to look stupid is a competitive advantage because most high-value opportunities require exploring ideas that sound too foolish for status-conscious people to consider, making these opportunities both valuable and accessible to anyone willing to ignore social proof.

quotes.quote: Often, the dumb solution is something any idiot could've come up with and the reason the problem hasn't been solved is because no one was willing to think the dumb thought until an idiot like me looked at the problem.

quotes.quote: When people said things like 'Forth is very productive', what I suspect they really meant was 'Forth makes me very productive and I have not considered how well this generalizes to people with different aptitudes or who are operating in different contexts'.
quotes.summary: Luu argues against generic "learn what I learned" career advice, advocating instead for identifying a few skills that match your personal aptitudes and learning them in environments with world-class experts. Rather than being a broad generalist or following popular recommendations (like learning Haskell or compilers), he suggests focusing on amplifying strengths through deliberate practice in conducive environments. He uses his own experience with skills like "having an adversarial mindset" and "looking at data" to illustrate how specialization and good learning environments trump breadth or fashionable technical choices.
quotes.key_insight: Effective skill development comes from identifying your natural aptitudes through trial and error, then deliberately practicing those specific skills in environments surrounded by world-class experts—not from following generic advice about what technologies or fields to learn.

quotes.quote: I think that people who are really good at something have too many bits of information in their head about how to do it for that information to really be compressible into a book, let alone a blog post.

quotes.quote: If, in the lifetime of the specialist team like the kernel team, a single person found something that persistently reduced TCO by 0.5%, that would pay for the team in perpetuity, and Twitter's kernel team has found many such changes.
quotes.summary: Companies at scale should invest in specialized in-house expertise even in areas not traditionally considered 'core' to their business, because these investments pay for themselves many times over through incident mitigation, performance optimizations, and long-term cost savings. Twitter's kernel team and JVM experts are cited as examples where single optimizations (like a 5-15% performance improvement from virtualizing compare-and-swap operations) generated returns far exceeding team costs in perpetuity. The common advice to 'buy vs build' and focus only on 'core competencies' is oversimplified—companies can strategically choose their comparative advantages and develop expertise that becomes core, as Apple did with CPU design.
quotes.key_insight: The conventional wisdom to outsource non-core functions and focus on comparative advantage ignores that at scale, companies can profitably develop deep expertise in 'specialized' areas like kernels and JVMs, with single optimizations often paying for entire teams in perpetuity while also choosing what becomes their comparative advantage.

quotes.quote: The two patches combined reduced the cost of typical major Twitter services using idiomatic Scala by 5% to 15%, paying for the JVM team in perpetuity many times over and that wasn't even the biggest win Flavio found that year.

quotes.quote: If anything, because measurement is, like writing, not generally valued, it's much easier to find high ROI measurement projects than high ROI building projects.
quotes.summary: The post argues that measurement and benchmarking projects are systematically undervalued compared to building software, despite often having higher ROI and broader impact. Luu demonstrates this through numerous examples where systematic measurement revealed widespread flaws—from Kyle Kingsbury's Jepsen work exposing catastrophic data loss in distributed databases to Consumer Reports' testing forcing improvements in car safety features. The root cause is that without rigorous third-party measurement, markets fail to select for quality because consumers can't distinguish good products from bad ones, and manufacturers optimize for appearance and marketing rather than actual performance.
quotes.key_insight: Markets systematically fail to produce quality products without rigorous independent measurement because consumers lack the information to distinguish good from bad, creating massive opportunities for high-impact work in unglamorous measurement and benchmarking projects.

quotes.quote: I find that it's generally true that, in areas where no one is publishing measurements/benchmarks of products, the products are generally sub-optimal, often in ways that are relatively straightforward to fix once measured.

quotes.quote: Brooks couldn't conceive of anyone building a project as complex as Win2k, which could really utilize faster hardware. Of course, using the tools and practices of Brooks's time, it was practically impossible to build as project as complex as Win2k
quotes.summary: Brooks's 1986 'No Silver Bullet' essay claimed that at least half of programming complexity is 'essential' (inherent to the problem) rather than 'accidental' (tool/technology-related), limiting possible productivity gains to at most 2x. This argument fails because it requires accurately predicting all future innovations—something Brooks demonstrably failed at, dismissing languages, AI, testing tools, and hardware improvements that later yielded massive gains. The essential/accidental complexity distinction is meaningless because problems with too much accidental complexity wouldn't even be attempted, making the 'essential' fraction effectively unbounded as tools improve.
quotes.key_insight: The distinction between essential and accidental complexity is fundamentally flawed because it can only account for problems imaginable with current technology—as tools improve, problems once deemed impossible become routine, revealing that nearly all complexity in practical programming work is accidental.

quotes.quote: I've personally never worked on a non-trivial problem that isn't completely dominated by accidental complexity, making the concept of essential complexity meaningless on any problem I've worked on that's worth discussing

quotes.quote: Long before computers were invented, elders have been telling the next generation that they've done everything that there is to be done and that the next generation won't be able to achieve more
quotes.summary: Brooks's 1986 'No Silver Bullet' essay claimed that at least half of programming complexity is 'essential' (inherent to the problem) rather than 'accidental' (tool/technology-related), limiting possible productivity gains to at most 2x. This argument fails because it requires accurately predicting all future innovations—something Brooks demonstrably failed at, dismissing languages, AI, testing tools, and hardware improvements that later yielded massive gains. The essential/accidental complexity distinction is meaningless because problems with too much accidental complexity wouldn't even be attempted, making the 'essential' fraction effectively unbounded as tools improve.

quotes.quote: Any time you have a benchmark that gets taken seriously, some people will start gaming the benchmark.
quotes.summary: Car manufacturers optimize their vehicles for specific crash test benchmarks rather than overall safety, as revealed when IIHS added small overlap crash tests in 2012 and 2018. When these new tests were introduced, only Volvo scored well without modifications—nearly all other manufacturers had poor initial scores but quickly improved them once the tests became public benchmarks. This pattern mirrors benchmark gaming in other industries and suggests most carmakers prioritize cost savings over safety improvements that aren't measured by official tests.
quotes.key_insight: Manufacturers systematically optimize for known benchmarks rather than actual safety, revealing that without continuous introduction of new tests covering previously unmeasured scenarios, safety improvements stagnate in areas that don't affect public scores.

quotes.quote: Despite the importance of small overlap crashes, from looking at the results when the IIHS added the driver-side and passenger-side small overlap tests in 2012 and 2018, it looks like almost all car manufacturers were optimizing for benchmark and not overall safety.

quotes.quote: I think this overestimates how much work it is to get a lot of value from tracing.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that distributed tracing can deliver high value without massive effort, contrary to common industry belief that it only works at Facebook/Google scale. Using Twitter's experience, he demonstrates how a small team (<2 person-years) transformed an underutilized trace-view system into a high-ROI analytics platform by restructuring trace data into queryable tables and building analysis tools on top. The key insight is that tracing data becomes valuable when you can run aggregations and queries across traces, not just view individual trace timelines.
quotes.key_insight: Distributed tracing becomes high-ROI infrastructure when trace data is structured for aggregation and querying rather than just individual trace viewing - a transformation that requires modest engineering effort, not Facebook-scale resources.

quotes.quote: Just having this set of tables, queryable with SQL queries... is enough for tracing to pay for itself, to go from being difficult to justify to being something that's obviously high value.

quotes.quote: I think writing about systems like this, that are just boring work is really underrated. A disproportionate number of posts and talks I read are about systems using hot technologies.
quotes.summary: Luu describes building LongTermMetrics (LTM), a system that stores and enables SQL queries over years of infrastructure metrics data, which took one day to build and immediately found millions in cost savings. The key insight is that storing just 0.01-0.1% of relevant metrics data indefinitely in a columnar format enables interactive analysis across all services and hosts, making it trivial to find misconfigurations and optimization opportunities. Rather than using sophisticated ML or real-time systems, LTM deliberately uses boring, proven technologies (Scalding, Presto, Parquet, HDFS) that required minimal effort to integrate with existing infrastructure. The post argues that simple, boring solutions often deliver better ROI than complex cutting-edge systems, but such work is underreported because it doesn't make good conference talks or resume items.
quotes.key_insight: Boring technology choices that prioritize minimal implementation effort and operational burden often deliver vastly better ROI than sophisticated systems, but industry incentives (resume building, promotions, conference talks) bias engineers toward complex solutions that turn simple projects into multi-year endeavors.

quotes.quote: In order to have a boring blog, the corporation has to actively stop engineers from putting interesting content out there.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu examines why his personal blog often gets more traffic than corporate engineering blogs from well-funded tech companies, despite those companies having more resources and greater incentive to succeed. Through interviews with companies that have compelling blogs (Cloudflare, Heap, Segment) and those that don't, he finds that successful corporate blogs share a lightweight approval process with direct executive support, while unsuccessful ones get bogged down in months-long approval cycles with multiple stakeholders who perceive only risk and no benefit from streamlining. The key difference is high-level executives willing to take responsibility and cut through bureaucratic risk aversion, enabling engineers to publish interesting technical content quickly.
quotes.key_insight: Corporate engineering blogs fail not from lack of talent or material, but because bureaucratic risk aversion without executive intervention transforms potentially interesting technical content into months-delayed, watered-down fluff that engineers hate writing and nobody wants to read.

quotes.quote: Saying that 'text' is a universal format is like saying that 'binary' is a universal format.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu examines how Unix command-line tools have dramatically increased in complexity since 1979, with commands like 'ls' growing from 11 options to 58 options. He argues this proliferation contradicts the Unix philosophy of 'do one thing well' but is actually inevitable given Unix's text-based design philosophy and the lack of structured data passing. While Unix pioneers like McIlroy decry this bloat, Luu contends the complexity was always going to exist somewhere—either in the tools themselves or pushed onto users who must manually parse and format text streams. The Unix philosophy worked fine for a small team in one room but fundamentally cannot scale to modern collaborative software development without creating inconsistency and accidental complexity.
quotes.key_insight: The Unix philosophy's design decisions that worked for a small colocated team in the 1970s fundamentally cannot scale to modern collaborative development, and the resulting complexity burden was always inevitable—the only question was whether it would be borne by tool maintainers or users.

quotes.quote: At least with tools that bundle up more functionality and options than is UNIX-y users can replace a gigantic set of wildly inconsistent tools with a merely large set of tools that, while inconsistent with each other, may have some internal consistency.

quotes.quote: The 'one weird trick' is that, for a lot of activities, being something like 10%-ile among people who practice can make you something like 90%-ile or 99%-ile among people who participate.
quotes.summary: The author argues that reaching 95th percentile performance in most activities is surprisingly achievable because most participants don't engage in deliberate practice or seek feedback. Using Overwatch as a detailed example, he demonstrates that even highly-ranked players constantly make basic, easily-correctable mistakes. The key insight is that the gap between casual participation and focused improvement is so large that modest effort—like reviewing your own performance, getting feedback, or practicing fundamentals—can yield dramatic percentile gains without requiring exceptional talent.
quotes.key_insight: Most people dramatically underestimate how achievable high percentile performance is because they conflate effort with deliberate practice—simply recording yourself, getting feedback, and fixing obvious mistakes can move you from average to top 5% in most activities.

quotes.quote: At 90%-ile and 95%-ile ranks in Overwatch, the vast majority of players will pretty much constantly make basic game losing mistakes.

quotes.quote: That means if an individual buying ACA insurance was going to earn $55k, they'd be better off reducing their income by $6440 and getting under the $48,560 subsidy ceiling than they are earning $55k.
quotes.summary: This post examines suspicious discontinuities across diverse domains - from tax policy to sports to drug prosecutions - where sharp thresholds create perverse incentives and non-smooth distributions. The author demonstrates how discontinuities lead to predictable gaming behavior: people losing money to qualify for health insurance subsidies, teachers inflating borderline test scores, prosecutors charging exactly at mandatory minimum thresholds, and birth month determining athletic careers. The common thread is that artificial bright-line rules create step functions in outcomes where smooth gradients would be more rational, and basic visualization techniques (histograms, scatterplots) reliably expose these patterns.
quotes.key_insight: Bright-line thresholds in policy, systems, and measurement create predictable discontinuities that incentivize gaming and produce outcomes opposite to their intended purpose, but simple visualization techniques can reliably expose these patterns.

quotes.quote: A tax system that encourages people to lose money, perhaps by funneling it to (on average) much wealthier options traders by buying put options, seems sub-optimal.

quotes.key_insight: Bright-line thresholds in policy, systems, and measurement create predictable discontinuities that incentivize gaming and produce outcomes opposite to their intended purpose, but simple visualization techniques can reliably expose these patterns.

quotes.quote: even though a decent fraction of the value I've provided for companies has been solving phone-screen level algorithms problems on the job, I can't pass algorithms interviews!
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that algorithms interviews fail to achieve their stated purpose of preventing inefficient code in production. Despite companies rigorously testing candidates on algorithms problems, he routinely finds trivial, interview-level inefficiencies in production code at major tech companies—bugs worth millions of dollars annually that persist because teams aren't incentivized to fix them. The real issue isn't hiring people who can't solve algorithms problems, but rather creating misaligned incentive structures that discourage engineers from applying that knowledge to make systems more efficient.
quotes.key_insight: The disconnect between what companies test for in interviews and what they incentivize on the job creates a situation where multi-million dollar inefficiencies persist despite hiring engineers who could easily fix them if given the right incentives.

quotes.quote: Many of the same things that make concurrent programming hard are the same things that make writing to files safely hard, so of course we should expect that writing to files is hard!
quotes.summary: This talk systematically dismantles the myth that file I/O is simple by examining failures at every layer of the storage stack. Luu demonstrates that writing a single file safely requires navigating filesystem-specific quirks, non-atomic operations, and ordering guarantees that even expert developers routinely get wrong. Through empirical evidence from academic research, he shows that filesystems drop errors at alarming rates, disks corrupt data millions of times more frequently than datasheets claim, and even 'power loss protection' features often fail in practice. The underlying issue isn't technical ignorance but economic misalignment: tech giants invest heavily in data integrity because they bear the costs of corruption, while consumer software vendors can externalize those costs to users.
quotes.key_insight: File I/O safety is not a solved problem but an economic one: the technical solutions exist and are deployed by companies who bear the cost of data loss, while consumer software remains unsafe because vendors can externalize corruption costs to users who lack the sophistication to assign blame.

quotes.quote: In less time than has been spent on a single large discussion, we could just run the experiment, so here it is.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu conducted a 339-game randomized experiment in Overwatch to test whether players receive different treatment based on perceived gender. Playing with masculine versus feminine usernames, he found that players with feminine names were told how to play or asked to switch characters at roughly 3x the rate (19% vs 6% in competitive mode), with qualitatively harsher criticism. Sexual comments directed at players were rare in both conditions, contradicting common assumptions but consistent with prior survey research. The study highlights how empirical testing can resolve longstanding online debates more efficiently than thousands of comment threads.
quotes.key_insight: Empirical experimentation with proper controls reveals that gendered harassment in online gaming manifests primarily through disproportionate unsolicited advice and qualitatively harsher criticism rather than overt sexual comments, demonstrating how thousands of hours of debate could be replaced by actually running the experiment.

quotes.quote: For example, the one time someone asked me to switch characters in the masculine condition, the request was a one sentence, polite, request. When using the feminine name, a typical case would involve 1-4 people calling me human garbage for most of the game and consoling themselves with the idea that the entire reason our team is losing is that I won't change characters.

quotes.quote: It's a bit absurd that a modern gaming machine running at 4,000x the speed of an apple 2, with a CPU that has 500,000x as many transistors (with a GPU that has 2,000,000x as many transistors) can maybe manage the same latency as an apple 2 in very carefully coded applications if we have a monitor with nearly 3x the refresh rate.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu empirically measured input-to-display latency across 40 years of computing devices (1977-2017) and found that most modern computers are significantly slower than machines from the 1970s and 80s. An Apple 2e from 1983 responds in 30ms while typical modern laptops range from 100-200ms. The culprit isn't raw processing power but architectural complexity: modern systems involve multiple process handoffs, buffering layers, and context switches that ancient machines simply didn't have, and the industry optimizes for throughput benchmarks rather than end-to-end latency.
quotes.key_insight: Despite 40 years of exponential hardware improvements, most modern computers feel slower than 1970s machines because the industry optimizes for measurable throughput benchmarks rather than unmeasured end-to-end user latency, demonstrating that you literally get what you measure.

quotes.quote: In practice, the solution to poor performance caused by 'excess' complexity is often to add more complexity.

quotes.quote: The really 'great' thing about a change like this is that it A/B tests incredibly well if you measure new feature 'engagement' by number of clicks because many users will accidentally save a story when they meant to hide it.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that software companies regularly break UI backwards compatibility in ways that exploit users' muscle memory, causing them to perform unintended actions. He contrasts this with the strict API backwards compatibility policies of systems like Linux and Windows, noting that UI changes are arguably even more problematic since users can't mechanically update their habits like code can be mechanically refactored. While maintaining UI compatibility creates constraints, Luu suggests that UI stability may be undervalued, pointing to successful products with frozen or dated interfaces, and proposes deprecation warnings for UI changes as a potential middle ground.
quotes.key_insight: Software companies systematically undervalue UI stability and backwards compatibility compared to API compatibility, despite UI changes being harder for users to adapt to and potentially more damaging to user experience and retention.

quotes.quote: A statement I commonly hear in tech-utopian circles is that some seeming inefficiency can't actually be inefficient because the market is efficient and inefficiencies will quickly be eliminated.
quotes.summary: Luu challenges the efficient market hypothesis by examining decision-making in baseball and board games, where outcomes are easily measurable and data is abundant. He demonstrates that even with clear statistical evidence showing decisions cost teams multiple wins per season (worth millions of dollars), managers persisted with demonstrably inferior strategies like excessive sac bunts, stolen base attempts, and intentional walks for decades. Despite financial incentives, accessible data, and rigorous analysis available since the 1970s, cultural resistance and tolerance for high-variance outcomes delayed adoption of evidence-based decision-making until the 2000s-2010s, suggesting similar or worse decision quality in less measurable business and engineering domains.
quotes.key_insight: Strong financial incentives and readily available data are insufficient to drive good decision-making when cultural inertia, high variance outcomes, and resistance to unconventional ideas allow decision-makers to dismiss clear statistical evidence for decades.

quotes.quote: At this point, we should expect that there are more than one billion devices that are two years out of date! Given Android's update model, we should expect approximately 0% of those devices to ever get updated to a modern version of Android.
quotes.summary: Using Android marketshare data from 2010-2017, this analysis demonstrates that Android devices are becoming increasingly outdated over time, with nearly half of the two billion active devices being at least two years behind on updates as of 2017. The data shows that new Android version adoption is slowing down, creating over one billion devices stuck on old versions that will likely never be updated. This contrasts sharply with iOS, where new versions achieve 50% adoption within months, and compares unfavorably even to Windows XP, which received twelve years of security updates.
quotes.key_insight: Data-driven analysis reveals that Android's update problem is worse than commonly understood and getting progressively worse over time, with the unprecedented scale of Android deployment creating billions of permanently outdated, potentially insecure devices that will persist for decades.

quotes.summary: Using Android marketshare data from 2010-2017, this analysis demonstrates that Android devices are becoming increasingly outdated over time, with nearly half of the two billion active devices being at least two years behind on updates as of 2017. The data shows that new Android version adoption is slowing down, creating over one billion devices stuck on old versions that will likely never be updated. This contrasts sharply with iOS, where new versions achieve 50% adoption within months, and compares unfavorably even to Windows XP, which received twelve years of security updates.
quotes.key_insight: Data-driven analysis reveals that Android's update problem is worse than commonly understood and getting progressively worse over time, with the unprecedented scale of Android deployment creating billions of permanently outdated, potentially insecure devices that will persist for decades.

quotes.quote: In general, my belief is that if someone makes performance claims without benchmarks, the claims probably aren't true, just like how code that isn't tested (or otherwise verified) should be assumed broken.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu empirically tests keyboard latency across 20+ keyboards and finds that most modern keyboards add 30-60ms of latency between keypress and USB output—more than the entire end-to-end pipeline of fast computers from the 1970s. Surprisingly, gaming keyboards marketed on speed claims like "1000Hz polling" and "8x faster response" perform no better than regular keyboards, while Apple's minimalist Magic keyboard (15ms) is the fastest tested. The research demonstrates that keyboard latency is both significant enough to be human-perceptible (studies show people detect latencies down to 2ms) and contributes meaningfully to the latency bloat that makes modern systems feel less responsive than decades-old machines despite vastly superior hardware.
quotes.key_insight: Marketing claims about performance are worthless without empirical measurement, and modern keyboard latency is a measurable, human-perceptible contributor to system responsiveness that could be easily fixed but isn't because manufacturers prioritize cost savings over user experience.

quotes.quote: More than 20% of flash drives develop uncorrectable errors in a four year period, 30-80% develop bad blocks and 2-7% of them develop bad chips.
quotes.summary: This post reproduces decade-old filesystem robustness studies to test how modern filesystems (ext4, btrfs, etc.) handle errors at the block device level. While write error propagation has improved since 2005, most filesystems still don't detect silent data corruption (except btrfs/zfs which use checksums). The investigation also examines internal error handling code and finds that many function return values that indicate write failures are still frequently ignored in the Linux kernel, though less so than in 2008.
quotes.key_insight: Despite a decade of development, most filesystems still lack basic data integrity checks and kernel code continues to ignore error conditions at concerning rates, suggesting that correctness work remains chronically underfunded and undervalued in both industry and open source.

quotes.quote: I find that this is common for explanations of concepts that could, but don't have to, use a lot of mathematical machinery...for people who are only interested in a single result, I think it's unfortunate that it's hard to find a relatively simple explanation that doesn't require any background.
quotes.summary: The post explains Sattolo's algorithm, which generates random permutations with exactly one cycle, contrasting it with the more common Fisher-Yates shuffle. Luu provides an accessible proof of why the algorithm works without requiring advanced mathematical machinery like Stirling numbers, motivated by finding that existing explanations were unnecessarily complex and that many people were misusing the algorithm. The post includes detailed proofs of correctness, discussion of derangements, and critiques common implementation mistakes in shuffling algorithms.
quotes.key_insight: Simple algorithms can have subtle correctness requirements that are often misunderstood in practice, and accessible explanations without heavy mathematical machinery are valuable for practitioners who need to understand a single concept rather than master an entire field.

quotes.quote: Since having one bit for every possible branch is too many bits to feasibly store, we'll keep a table of some number of branches we've seen and their last results.
quotes.summary: This post provides a technical deep-dive into CPU branch prediction, explaining why modern processors predict branch outcomes and how various prediction algorithms evolved from simple schemes to sophisticated multi-level predictors. Luu methodically walks through increasingly complex prediction strategies—from always predicting 'taken' (70% accuracy) to hybrid predictors combining local and global state (96% accuracy)—demonstrating how each improvement addresses specific weaknesses in prior approaches. The central argument is that CPUs are actually simpler and more understandable than most programmers assume, because hardware complexity is constrained by physical costs, performance overhead, and verification requirements that don't limit software in the same way.
quotes.key_insight: Modern CPUs are more conceptually accessible than programmers assume because physical and economic constraints force hardware designs toward elegant simplicity, whereas software complexity is limited only by human imagination and organizational capability.

quotes.quote: The idea that computers respond quickly to input, so quickly that humans can't notice the latency, is the most common performance-related fallacy I hear from professional programmers.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu systematically measures terminal emulator latency and finds that most terminals add 30-100ms of delay from keypress to display, enough to be perceptible to users. He argues the terminal ecosystem is optimized for the wrong benchmarks—stdout throughput and memory usage—when latency and responsiveness matter far more for user experience. His measurements show significant variance across terminals and demonstrate that tail latency under load becomes even more problematic, with 99.9th percentile latencies reaching levels comparable to cross-country network roundtrips.
quotes.key_insight: Terminal developers optimize for easily measured but largely irrelevant metrics like stdout throughput while ignoring the latency that actually degrades user experience, demonstrating how misaligned benchmarks lead to misguided optimization efforts.

quotes.quote: If you wanted to get rich, how would you do it? I think your best bet would be to start or join a startup. That's been a reliable way to get rich for hundreds of years" and "Risk and reward are always proportionate." This risk-reward assertion is used to back the claim that people can make more money, in expectation, by joining startups and taking risky equity packages than they can by taking jobs that pay cash or cash plus public equity. However, the premise — that risk and reward are always proportionate — isn't true in the general case.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu challenges the common startup pitch that equity compensation has higher expected value than cash packages from large tech companies. He argues that if startup options were truly as valuable as claimed, companies could sell more equity to VCs and pay employees in cash instead. Through detailed analysis of how employee stock options differ from investor preferred stock (liquidation preferences, dilution, exercise windows), tax implications, and historical data showing VC funds don't outperform the market, he demonstrates that most startup equity packages are worth significantly less than advertised. The piece systematically dismantles both cynical and non-cynical justifications for option-heavy compensation, concluding that the arrangement primarily benefits founders and investors at employees' expense.
quotes.key_insight: The persistent use of stock options over cash compensation in startups is better explained by information asymmetry and the ability to obscure true compensation value than by any legitimate financial or incentive-alignment benefit to employees.

quotes.quote: I'm deeply skeptical of broad claims that come from microbenchmarks unless the author has a demonstrated, deep, understanding of benchmarking their particular domain, and even then I'll ask why they believe their result generalizes.
quotes.summary: Widely cited claims from Apple researcher Bruce Tognazzini that mice are faster than keyboards for all tasks are based on unreproducible studies and one bogus microbenchmark. When the author actually tests various tasks (search-and-replace, text selection, scrolling), results are mixed and predictable: keyboards excel at some tasks, mice at others. The literature on keyboard vs. mouse speed is plagued by issues including outdated studies from the 1980s, artificial microbenchmarks, vague descriptions, and tests on unskilled users that don't generalize to experienced programmers.
quotes.key_insight: The influential Apple studies claiming mice are universally faster than keyboards are based on unreproducible research and artificial microbenchmarks that ignore how skilled users actually work, while empirical testing shows the obvious truth that each input method excels at different tasks.

quotes.quote: The e -> | replacement task described is not only a microbenchmark, it's a bizarrely artificial microbenchmark.

quotes.quote: It's entirely possible that the mysterious studies Tog's org spent $50M on prove that the mouse is faster than the keyboard for all tasks other than raw text input, but there doesn't appear to be enough information to tell what the actual studies were.
quotes.summary: Widely cited claims from Apple researcher Bruce Tognazzini that mice are faster than keyboards for all tasks are based on unreproducible studies and one bogus microbenchmark. When the author actually tests various tasks (search-and-replace, text selection, scrolling), results are mixed and predictable: keyboards excel at some tasks, mice at others. The literature on keyboard vs. mouse speed is plagued by issues including outdated studies from the 1980s, artificial microbenchmarks, vague descriptions, and tests on unskilled users that don't generalize to experienced programmers.
quotes.key_insight: The influential Apple studies claiming mice are universally faster than keyboards are based on unreproducible research and artificial microbenchmarks that ignore how skilled users actually work, while empirical testing shows the obvious truth that each input method excels at different tasks.

quotes.quote: This result is so boring that it would barely be worth mentioning if not for the large groups of people who believe that either the keyboard is always faster than the mouse or vice versa.

quotes.quote: When I was at Google, someone told me a story about a time that 'they' completed a big optimization push only to find that measured page load times increased. When they dug into the data, they found that the reason load times had increased was that they got a lot more traffic from Africa after doing the optimizations.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu documents how modern web bloat makes the internet largely unusable for people with slow connections, demonstrating through extensive testing that most popular websites fail to load on dialup-speed connections despite being text-based. He tests various sites across connection speeds from fiber to 16kbps satellite, finding that even Google takes 90+ seconds on dialup while many commercial sites fail entirely. The post argues this isn't just about developing countries—millions of Americans still lack broadband, and the problem is often caused by easily fixable anti-optimizations in popular themes and frameworks.
quotes.key_insight: Web performance optimization is treated as optional nicety for nerds, but the reality is that poor optimization excludes millions of users with slow connections from accessing fundamentally text-based content, and most sites could achieve 10-20x improvements with minimal effort by just avoiding blatant anti-optimizations.

quotes.quote: The flaw in the 'page weight doesn't matter because average speed is fast' is that if you average the connection of someone in my apartment building (which is wired for 1Gbps internet) and someone on 56k dialup, you get an average speed of 500 Mbps. That doesn't mean the person on dialup is actually going to be able to load a 5MB website.

quotes.quote: When people imagine how long it should take to build something, they're often imagining a team that works perfectly and spends 100% of its time coding. But that's impossible to scale up.
quotes.summary: The post challenges the common misconception that large tech companies are bloated and wasteful by explaining why products like Google Search require thousands of engineers despite seeming simple on the surface. Luu demonstrates that building an MVP in a weekend is vastly different from building a production system at scale, which requires extensive work on performance optimization, internationalization, security, error handling, and countless revenue-driving features that users don't even notice. He argues that companies rationally keep hiring engineers as long as each additional engineer generates more value through cost savings, performance improvements, or revenue-increasing features than they cost to employ.
quotes.key_insight: The vast majority of engineering work at large tech companies goes into optimization, internationalization, reliability, and incremental features that each add small revenue gains - work that's invisible to outsiders but essential for profitability at scale.

quotes.quote: Distributed systems are considered hard because real systems might drop something like 0.1% of messages, corrupt an even smaller percentage of messages, and see latencies in the microsecond to millisecond range. When I talk to higher-ups and compare what they think they're saying to what my coworkers think they're saying, I find that the rate of lost messages is well over 50%, every message gets corrupted, and latency can be months or years.

quotes.quote: For reliable systems, error handling is more work than the happy path. The same thing is true for large services -- all of this stuff that people don't think of as 'real' work is more work than the core service.

quotes.quote: The field is too broad for almost any topic to be required reading for all programmers, and even if a topic is that important, people's learning preferences differ too much for any book on that topic to be the best book on the topic for all people.
quotes.summary: Luu critiques the common "must-read programming books" lists as nonsense given the field's breadth and diverse learning styles, instead offering nuanced recommendations based on actual reading experience. He systematically covers algorithms, operating systems, computer architecture, game theory, and hardware fundamentals, providing clear guidance on when to read comprehensive textbooks versus practical introductions. Throughout, he debunks the myth that certain "classic" books like CLRS or Peopleware are universally appropriate, arguing that many widely-recommended texts are impractical for self-study or mismatched to their purported audience.
quotes.key_insight: The best technical books teach you how to reason about tradeoffs and develop intuition rather than achieve encyclopedic coverage, and the most widely-recommended "classics" are often terrible choices for self-study despite their prestige.

quotes.quote: This book somehow manages to make it into half of these 'N books all programmers must read' lists despite being so comprehensive and rigorous that almost no practitioners actually read the entire thing.

quotes.quote: I can think of multiple production systems at large companies that are giving up 10x to 100x in performance which are operating at a scale where even a 2x difference in performance could pay a VP's salary -- all because people didn't think through the performance implications of their design.

quotes.quote: On topics I'm familiar with, while it's rare that a thread will have even a single comment that's well-informed, when those comments appear, they usually float to the top.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that while HN comments are mostly wrong and often mean-spirited, it remains the best public technical forum available because well-informed comments, though rare, tend to rise to the top. He curates dozens of excellent HN comments across topics like software engineering, business operations, and tech industry dynamics to demonstrate the hidden value buried in comment threads. The post explores what makes HN's comment quality superior to other forums, attributing it to careful ranking algorithms that penalize flamebait and heavily weight user flags, though the ultimate cause may simply be network effects and concentration of expertise.
quotes.key_insight: HN's value lies not in its average comment quality but in its unique ability to surface rare expert insights to the top, though this valuable knowledge gets lost over time unlike blog posts.

quotes.quote: Most people realize by their 30s that prestige is a sucker's game; it's a way of inducing people to do things that aren't much fun and they wouldn't really want to do on their own, by lauding them with accolades from people they don't really care about.
