quotes.quote: It's actually quite common for prospective employers to have better information than current employers!
quotes.summary: Dan Luu challenges Joel Spolsky's famous claim that great developers are nearly impossible to hire because they're never on the market. Luu argues this model contradicts itself—if great developers are easy to identify, companies would simply poach them. Through empirical observations, he demonstrates that the hiring market isn't a 'market for lemons' as commonly believed, but rather that hiring difficulties stem from four fixable problems: paying too little, filtering out good candidates through arbitrary criteria, seeking rare skill combinations, or being more dysfunctional than management realizes. The real scarcity isn't great developers, but rather great teams and environments worth joining.
quotes.key_insight: The hiring crisis in tech is largely self-inflicted—companies systematically underpay and filter out qualified candidates using arbitrary criteria, then complain about talent scarcity while great developers struggle to find good teams worth joining.

quotes.quote: If you join a random team, you're overwhelmingly likely to join a team that has a lot of churn. Additionally, if you know of a good team, it's likely to be full.

quotes.quote: If you believe in the common 'you either have it or you don't' narrative about programmers, I definitely don't have it. And yet, I now make a living programming, and people seem to be pretty happy with the work I do.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu recounts his circuitous 15+ year journey into programming, starting with failed childhood attempts, studying electrical engineering and math instead of CS, and working as a hardware engineer for a decade before transitioning to software. He challenges the 'you either have it or you don't' narrative about programming talent, arguing that meta-skills like debugging persistence, bug tracking, learning how to approach hard problems, and working with mentors were more valuable than any specific technical knowledge. The post emphasizes that successful programming careers rarely follow the stereotypical 'whiz kid' path and that luck, serendipity, and supportive environments play major roles that are difficult to replicate as career advice.
quotes.key_insight: The path to becoming a successful programmer is far more varied and luck-dependent than the industry's 'natural talent' narrative suggests, and the meta-skills of persistence, learning how to learn, and working with great mentors matter more than any specific technical knowledge or educational pedigree.

quotes.quote: I find that I have a meta-skill set that I use all the time. There's nothing profound about the meta-skill set, but because I often work in new (to me) problem domains, I find my meta skillset to be more valuable than my actual skills.

quotes.quote: Because the mechanism for compensation increases has been arbitrary (FB could not exist, or the person who's in total control of FB, Zuckerberg, could decide on different compensation policy), it's quite arbitrary that programmer pay is as good as it is.
quotes.summary: This post examines whether developer compensation is becoming bimodal like it has in law and consulting, where income distributions show two distinct peaks rather than a smooth distribution. While fields like law have clear bimodal patterns driven by tournament-like up-or-out structures and lockstep pay scales, programmer compensation appears to have high dispersion but lacks the sharp separation into distinct modes. The author struggles to find a convincing explanation for why programmers are paid so well compared to other engineering fields that require similar skills, noting that common explanations like 'software is eating the world' or 'programming is hard' apply equally to fields that pay much less.
quotes.key_insight: High programmer compensation appears to be driven by contingent historical factors like the collapse of wage-fixing agreements rather than inherent properties of the field, and common explanations fail because they apply equally to lower-paid engineering disciplines.

quotes.summary: This post examines whether developer compensation is becoming bimodal like it has in law and consulting, where income distributions show two distinct peaks rather than a smooth distribution. quotes.quote: I've spent more time debugging some individual non-deterministic bugs (weeks or months) than on all other bug types combined.
quotes.summary: Luu examines the academic literature on concurrency bugs in both single-machine and distributed systems, finding that while most bugs are simple by count, non-deterministic concurrency bugs consume disproportionate debugging time (weeks to months per bug). He surveys numerous academic tools for detecting these bugs and notes a frustrating pattern: most published research tools are unavailable or unmaintained, while the most impactful real-world tools (TSan, Jepsen, AFL) use simpler, older techniques prioritizing practicality over novelty. The gap between academic research and usable tooling represents a major missed opportunity for industry impact.
quotes.key_insight: The most impactful bug-finding tools succeed not through algorithmic novelty but through relentless focus on usability and availability, revealing a systemic failure in academic computer science to translate research into practical tools that practitioners can actually use.

quotes.quote: One interesting incident occurred in the open source Chrome browser. Up to 15% of known crashes were attributed to just one bug which proved difficult to understand - the Chrome engineers spent over 6 months tracking this bug without success. On the other hand, the TSAN V1 team found the reason for this bug in a 30 minute run.

quotes.quote: This is one of those 'N technical things every programmer must read' lists, except that 'programmer' is way too broad a term and the styles of writing people find helpful for them are too different for any such list to contain a non-zero number of items.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu curates a comprehensive list of programming blogs worth reading, organized by author with brief descriptions of their content focus and writing style. Rather than prescribing a universal reading list, he acknowledges that different programmers have different learning styles and interests. The post emphasizes the decline of blog discovery through blogrolls and calls for readers to share recommendations, noting that even popular blogs can fly under the radar.
quotes.key_insight: The fragmentation of the programming blogosphere means valuable technical writing is increasingly hard to discover, and curated lists with context about writing style and difficulty level help readers find content that matches their learning preferences.

quotes.quote: If a human operator needs to touch your system during normal operations, you have a bug. The definition of normal changes as your systems grow.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu's detailed notes on Google's SRE book reveal how Google approaches reliability through software engineering principles rather than traditional operations. Key insights include error budgets that align incentive between SRE and product teams (allowing teams to 'spend' unreliability to move faster), the 50% cap on operational work to force automation, and blameless postmortems. The notes emphasize that achieving reliability requires treating it as a first-class engineering problem with concrete metrics, automation, and thoughtful tradeoffs rather than pursuing perfect uptime.
quotes.key_insight: Reliability is fundamentally an engineering problem requiring quantifiable metrics, automation, and explicit tradeoffs rather than heroic effort or perfect uptime - teams should purposefully consume error budget to maximize velocity while maintaining acceptable reliability.

quotes.quote: You are competing for talent with the wealthiest (or most overfunded) tech companies in the market.
quotes.summary: Tech companies claim to hire 'only the best' but systematically reject talented engineers for superficial reasons like using the 'wrong' tech stack, working as contractors, or lacking brand-name credentials. This strategy forces them to compete for the same tiny pool of candidates that Google and Facebook are willing to pay $200k+ for new grads, while passing over equally capable engineers who are undervalued by the market. The author argues this is both economically irrational and creates path-dependent career outcomes where random early-career events follow people for decades, similar to how graduating during a recession permanently impacts lifetime earnings.
quotes.key_insight: Tech companies irrationally compete for the same overpriced 'trendy' candidates while rejecting equally talented engineers for superficial reasons, when they could achieve better results more cheaply by hiring undervalued people and investing in training, tools, and processes.

quotes.quote: Sampling profilers, the most common performance debugging tool, are notoriously bad at debugging problems caused by tail latency because they aggregate events into averages. But tail latency is, by definition, not average.
quotes.summary: Traditional sampling profilers like perf are widely used but fundamentally inadequate for debugging tail latency and rare performance problems because they aggregate events into averages and sample too infrequently (typically 1kHz). The post demonstrates through several Google case studies how tracing frameworks that capture every event at much higher resolution (with ~1% overhead) can reveal critical performance bugs that sampling profilers miss—including lock contention causing 50us waits, batched reindexing causing 1800ms email delays, and CPU quota throttling causing 250ms periodic stalls across entire machines. While Google's internal tracing tools can debug these problems effectively, the public tooling ecosystem remains fragmented and high-overhead, leaving most engineers stuck with profilers that can't see the performance problems that increasingly matter.
quotes.key_insight: The dominant performance debugging paradigm—sampling profilers—is fundamentally mismatched to the problems that matter most (tail latency), yet superior tracing approaches remain inaccessible outside companies like Google, creating a tooling gap that causes critical performance bugs to go undetected for years.

quotes.quote: We know from the Nyquist theorem that, to observe a signal with some frequency, X, we have to sample with a rate at least 2X. There are a lot of factors of performance that have a frequency higher than 1kHz (e.g., CPU p-state changes), so we should expect that we're unable to directly observe a lot of things that affect performance with perf or other traditional sampling profilers.

quotes.quote: If this CPU microcode hang had been found by one of these black hats, there would have been major carnage for most cloud hosted services at the most inconvenient possible time.
quotes.summary: Intel experienced numerous severe CPU bugs in 2015 despite strong financial performance, including bugs that allowed VMs to crash host systems and widespread system hangs from common benchmarking tools. The author argues these bugs represent a systemic shift at Intel away from rigorous validation toward faster development cycles, driven by competitive pressure from ARM in the mobile market. Evidence suggests Intel's validation teams were explicitly told to move faster and cut validation times around 2013-2014, coinciding with an exodus of verification engineers and a measurable increase in serious bugs with security implications.
quotes.key_insight: Intel's competitive pressure from ARM led to a documented cultural shift around 2013 that prioritized development velocity over validation rigor, transforming CPU bugs from rare annoyances into regular security vulnerabilities with catastrophic potential for cloud infrastructure.

quotes.quote: We need to move faster. Validation at Intel is taking much longer than it does for our competition. We need to do whatever we can to reduce those times... we can't live forever in the shadow of the early 90's FDIV bug, we need to move on.

quotes.quote: The data are clear that humans are really bad at taking the time to do things that are well understood to incontrovertibly reduce the risk of rare but catastrophic events. We will rationalize that taking shortcuts is the right, reasonable thing to do.
quotes.summary: Luu explores the phenomenon of "normalization of deviance" in software engineering, where dangerous practices become accepted as normal over time despite their risks. Drawing parallels from healthcare, aviation, and other fields, he argues that tech companies routinely normalize practices that would be considered shocking elsewhere—from broken builds to non-existent security to rewarding heroism over prevention. The industry lacks the systematic approach to learning from failures that other fields have developed, instead relying on cargo-culting practices from successful companies without understanding the context.
quotes.key_insight: Software engineering lacks systematic mechanisms to prevent the normalization of deviance that other fields have developed, relying instead on cargo-culting practices and only learning from catastrophic failures rather than near-misses or chronic dysfunction.

quotes.summary: Luu explores the phenomenon of "normalization of deviance" in software engineering, where dangerous practices become accepted as normal over time despite their risks. Drawing parallels from healthcare, aviation, and other fields, he argues that tech companies routinely normalize practices that would be considered shocking elsewhere—from broken builds to non-existent security to rewarding heroism over prevention. The industry lacks the systematic approach to learning from failures that other fields have developed, instead relying on cargo-culting practices from successful companies without understanding the context.
quotes.key_insight: Software engineering lacks systematic mechanisms to prevent the normalization of deviance that other fields have developed, relying instead on cargo-culting practices and only learning from catastrophic failures rather than near-misses or chronic dysfunction.

quotes.quote: Roll d100... 0~70: Your equity grant is worth nothing. 71~94: Your equity grant is worth a lump sum of money which makes you about as much money as you gave up working for the startup. 95~99: Your equity grant is a life changing amount of money. 100: You worked at the next Google, and are rich beyond the dreams of avarice. Perceptive readers will note that 100 does not actually show up on a d100.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu systematically dismantles the common startup mythology by analyzing compensation, work quality, and career development at big tech companies versus startups. Using concrete numbers, he demonstrates that senior engineers at major tech companies can earn a lifetime's median US income in just 6-7 years ($250k+/year), while startup equity has roughly a 0.5% chance of meaningful payoff for early employees. He argues that big companies now offer both superior compensation and access to technically interesting problems at unprecedented scale, though acknowledges the trade-offs vary by individual interests and circumstances.
quotes.key_insight: The conventional wisdom that startups offer better compensation and more interesting work than big tech companies is outdated - the 2010s tech boom has inverted the risk-reward calculation, with big companies now offering both higher expected value compensation and access to technically challenging problems at unprecedented scale.

quotes.quote: The only difference between then and now is that I took a job at a big company. When I took that job, the common story I heard at orientation was basically 'I never thought I'd be able to get a job at Google, but a recruiter emailed me and I figured I might as well respond'.

quotes.quote: Files are hard.
quotes.summary: Desktop applications routinely fail to handle file operations correctly, leading to data corruption that even Gmail avoids despite being distributed. The post systematically demonstrates that writing files safely requires navigating a minefield of filesystem-specific behaviors, undocumented semantics, poorly-handled disk errors, and reordering issues that mirror the challenges of concurrent programming. Most developers underestimate this complexity, resulting in widespread bugs even in critical applications like version control systems, databases, and email clients.
quotes.key_insight: File I/O is fundamentally a concurrent programming problem with filesystem-specific reordering and atomicity semantics, but unlike multithreading it's treated casually by developers, leading to pervasive data corruption bugs that could be caught with basic testing and static analysis.

quotes.quote: Correctly reasoning about re-ordering behavior and inserting barriers correctly is hard. But even though shared memory concurrency is considered a hard problem that requires great care, writing to files isn't treated the same way, even though it's actually harder in a number of ways.

quotes.quote: Making mistakes and then fixing them is common in every successful engineering organization. If you're going to cargo cult an engineering practice, you should at least cargo cult current engineering practices, not something that was done in 1999.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu systematically refutes Jeff Atwood's arguments against using ECC memory in servers, demonstrating that Atwood's reasoning is based on outdated information (Google's 1999 decision, which Google itself reversed), misunderstandings of hard vs. soft errors, and flawed market logic. Luu provides empirical evidence that memory errors are common enough to cause significant silent data corruption, with failure rates of 0.5+ faults per server per year even with conservative estimates, and that ECC provides crucial error detection capabilities that prevent catastrophic data corruption. He argues that the case for ECC is actually stronger for consumers than servers, since consumers have fewer sophisticated error detection mechanisms, though the primary reason consumers don't use ECC is lack of awareness rather than lack of benefit. The post demonstrates through multiple real-world examples (Google's search index corruption, Sun's cache reliability issues) that silent memory corruption is a persistent problem that ECC effectively mitigates at reasonable cost.
quotes.key_insight: ECC memory is a cost-effective necessity for preventing silent data corruption in both servers and consumer systems, and the primary barrier to consumer adoption is lack of awareness rather than lack of benefit, as empirical evidence shows memory errors cause 10-20% of real-world application crashes even with modern hardware.

quotes.quote: A major reason consumers don't generally use ECC isn't that it isn't worth it for them, it's that they just have no idea how to attribute crashes and data corruption when they happen.

quotes.quote: The round-trip latency is so much lower than the seek time of a disk that we can dis-aggregate storage and distribute it anywhere in the datacenter without noticeable performance degradation, giving applications the appearance of having infinite disk space.
quotes.summary: Hardware latency improvements, particularly in networking and storage, fundamentally change optimal system architecture. As network round-trip times have dropped from 500μs to 10s of μs and NVRAM access times have decreased to 15μs, it's now feasible to disaggregate storage across datacenters without performance penalties—providing applications with effectively "infinite disk." This shift means systems designed for 2005-era latencies are no longer optimal; applications that relied on local caching for 1ms latencies now carry counter-productive overhead when network latencies drop below 1μs. The same economics that enable datacenter disaggregation will inevitably extend to consumer cloud storage as bandwidth costs continue their exponential decline.
quotes.key_insight: Hardware performance ratios—not just absolute speeds—determine optimal system architectures, and as network/storage latency ratios shift by orders of magnitude, yesterday's performance optimizations become tomorrow's bottlenecks.

quotes.quote: Lampson's comment was that when they came up with threading, locks, and conditional variables at PARC, they thought they were creating something that programmers could use to take advantage of parallelism, but that they now have decades of evidence that they were wrong.
quotes.summary: Luu revisits Butler Lampson's 1999 assessment of computer science research to evaluate what's changed 16 years later. He methodically analyzes each category (Yes/Maybe/No) from Lampson's original talk, finding that nearly all the 1999 'Yes' items remain fundamental today, while seven formerly uncertain or failed technologies (like garbage collection, distributed systems, and RPC) became mainstream successes. Only RISC moved from 'Maybe' to 'No' as x86 dominated despite predictions of its demise. The analysis demonstrates that the landscape has changed substantially within a single career, making long-term predictions about research impact fundamentally unreliable.
quotes.key_insight: The rapid transformation from research concepts to mainstream adoption within a single career span makes long-term technological predictions essentially impossible, suggesting humility about dismissing today's academic research areas as impractical.

quotes.quote: A clean RISC ISA is a beautiful thing. I sometimes re-read Dick Sites's explanation of the Alpha design just to admire it, but it turns out beauty isn't really critical for the commercial success of an ISA.

quotes.quote: Typical server utilization is between 10% and 50%. Google has demonstrated 90% utilization without impacting latency SLAs.
quotes.summary: Intel added cache partitioning (Cache Allocation Technology/CAT) to enable higher server utilization by allowing latency-sensitive and batch workloads to coexist on the same machines. Research shows datacenter workloads only need 4-6MB of LLC despite chips having 12-30MB caches, and spend much of their time stalled on memory despite low bandwidth utilization. By partitioning cache alongside other resource isolation mechanisms (CPU pinning, network quotas, power limiting), systems like Google's Heracles achieve 90% utilization compared to typical 10-50% utilization, translating to hundreds of millions in cost savings.
quotes.key_insight: Modern datacenter economics makes even small utilization improvements worth massive engineering effort, and empirical measurement reveals that datacenter workloads have fundamentally different resource usage patterns than traditional benchmarks assume, enabling novel hardware features like cache partitioning to unlock dramatic efficiency gains.

quotes.quote: Datacenter workloads operate on such large data sets that it's often impossible to fit the dataset in RAM on a single machine, let alone in cache, making a larger LLC not particularly useful.

quotes.key_insight: Modern datacenter economics makes even small utilization improvements worth massive engineering effort, and empirical measurement reveals that datacenter workloads have fundamentally different resource usage patterns than traditional benchmarks assume, enabling novel hardware features like cache partitioning to unlock dramatic efficiency gains.

quotes.quote: SPEC is basically irrelevant as a benchmark now. It's somewhat dated as a workstation benchmark, and simply completely inappropriate as a benchmark for servers, office machines, gaming machines, dumb terminals, laptops, and mobile devices.

quotes.quote: The job completion rate slowed down from 172 jobs per hour to 1 job per hour, effectively killing the entire cluster.
quotes.summary: Luu examines how partially-degraded hardware ('limping' nodes) can catastrophically impact distributed systems performance far more than completely failed nodes. Through analysis of research by Thanh Do et al., he shows that a single slow disk or network card can cause 100x+ performance degradation across entire clusters in systems like Hadoop, HDFS, and HBase. While these failures are relatively rare (3% of hardware issues), they expose fundamental design flaws in tail-tolerance mechanisms and cascading failure prevention that have persisted since MapReduce's original 2004 paper.
quotes.key_insight: Distributed systems' tail-tolerance mechanisms often assume binary failure states (working/dead) and catastrophically fail against the more insidious case of degraded-but-functioning hardware, a design oversight that has persisted for over a decade despite being documented in foundational papers.

quotes.quote: It's a no-win proposition: if you're right, hindsight bias makes it look like you're pointing out the obvious. And most predictions are wrong.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu reviews Steve Yegge's 2004 tech predictions, finding them remarkably accurate despite seeming obvious in hindsight. Most predictions were directionally correct, including the rise of JavaScript, cloud hosting, NoSQL databases, and mobile computing, with only the Lisp prediction missing significantly. The analysis demonstrates how good predictions suffer from hindsight bias—they seem obvious after the fact but were controversial when made—and shows how Yegge underestimated market inertia in his timing but correctly identified major industry trends.
quotes.key_insight: Accurate predictions about technology trends suffer from a paradox: hindsight bias makes them seem obvious after the fact, obscuring how controversial and non-obvious they were when originally made, while the main failure mode is underestimating how long market inertia delays even correct directional insights.

quotes.quote: If you care about building robust systems, the error checking code is the main code!
quotes.summary: Dan Luu examines common patterns in postmortems from major tech companies, identifying that most critical failures stem from a few recurring categories: error handling bugs (92% of critical failures involve incorrectly handled errors), configuration changes (responsible for ~50% of global outages despite being seen as "obviously" risky), hardware failures including silent data corruption, and process bugs that enable catastrophic human errors. Despite these failure modes being "obvious" to engineers, companies repeatedly fail to invest adequately in testing error paths, staging config changes, and automating risky manual operations, leading to the same types of outages over and over.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between what's "obvious" about system reliability and what actually gets implemented is enormous—companies repeatedly suffer outages from well-known failure modes because they don't invest proportionally in testing error paths, staging config changes, and automating manual procedures.

quotes.quote: No clueful person is going to ever download something from SourceForge again.
quotes.summary: SourceForge destroyed sixteen years of user trust by taking over project admin accounts and forcibly injecting adware into installers like GIMP, transitioning from optional to mandatory malware bundling. Slashdot, owned by the same parent company, compounded the damage by censoring all stories about the SourceForge scandal for a week while it spread across every other tech publication. This represents a textbook case of how quickly companies can destroy brand value through short-term revenue grabs and ineffective damage control that only makes things worse.
quotes.key_insight: Corporate panic over declining revenue can lead to catastrophically bad decision-making where management destroys long-term brand value through both aggressive short-term monetization and ineffective cover-ups that make the situation worse.

quotes.quote: With a monorepo, you just refactor the API and all of its callers in one commit. That's not always trivial, but it's much easier than it would be with lots of small repos.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that monorepos (single large repositories containing all of a company's code) offer significant practical advantages over managing multiple smaller repositories. The primary benefits include simplified organization and navigation, easier dependency management with universal versioning, better tooling due to unified structure, and the ability to make atomic cross-project changes and refactorings. While acknowledging downsides exist, Luu contends that companies like Google, Facebook, and Twitter have adopted monorepos for sound engineering reasons, not ignorance of alternatives.
quotes.key_insight: Monorepos aren't an ignorant mistake by large tech companies but a deliberate engineering choice that trades different constraints for substantial gains in code navigation, dependency management, tooling simplicity, and the ability to make atomic cross-project changes.

quotes.quote: SVN, hg, git, etc solve the problem of atomic cross-file changes; monorepos solve the same problem across projects.

quotes.quote: Running a competing search engine and preventing a Google monopoly is hard enough without having sites ban non-Google bots.
quotes.summary: Many websites accidentally create a Google search monopoly by blocking all web crawlers except Googlebot in their robots.txt files. Bell Labs exemplifies this by blocking archive.org and all search engines except Google and the outdated msnbot, making their content inaccessible to alternative search engines like Yandex and preventing historical archiving. This widespread practice, often unintentional, makes it nearly impossible for competing search engines to operate and has led to some crawlers simply ignoring robots.txt entirely.
quotes.key_insight: Web developers are inadvertently reinforcing Google's search monopoly through careless robots.txt configurations that block all crawlers except Googlebot, a practice so widespread it's rendering the robots.txt standard itself obsolete.

quotes.quote: We used to build steel mills near cheap sources of power, but now that's where we build datacenters.
quotes.summary: While power efficiency in computing is often discussed in the context of mobile devices and chip scaling limits, datacenter power consumption represents a massive and underappreciated cost driver. Power costs compound through infrastructure losses (UPS, voltage conversion, PSUs) and cooling requirements, with every watt of server power requiring 1-2 additional watts for support systems. The total cost of power and cooling infrastructure plus operational power use accounts for 33% of lifetime datacenter costs, rivaling the cost of servers themselves and fundamentally reshaping where and how we build computing infrastructure.
quotes.key_insight: Datacenter power consumption has become so significant that power costs (infrastructure plus usage) rival server hardware costs, fundamentally shifting where computing infrastructure gets built and making power efficiency a first-order business concern rather than just a technical optimization.

quotes.quote: When I'm doing boring work that's basically bottlenecked on the speed at which I can write boilerplate, it feels much nicer to use an interesting language (like F#), which lets me cut down on the amount of time spent writing boilerplate. But when I'm doing interesting work, the boilerplate is a rounding error and I don't mind using a boring language like Java.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu argues that "boring" mainstream languages like C, C++, and Java are underrated and actually more valuable for serious work than "interesting" fancy languages. While interesting languages can reduce boilerplate for routine tasks, when tackling genuinely difficult problems, domain knowledge matters far more than language features. The most impressive software systems - from operating systems to distributed databases to major web services - are overwhelmingly built in these supposedly boring languages, and the best engineers typically have deep domain expertise rather than expertise in exotic languages.
quotes.key_insight: The value of a programming language for difficult work lies not in its features for reducing boilerplate, but in the quality of engineers and systems it gives you access to - and empirically, the world's most impressive software is built in "boring" languages by people with deep domain expertise.

quotes.quote: Why do I work in boring languages? Because that's what the people I want to work with use, and what the systems I want to work on are written in.

While interesting languages can reduce boilerplate for routine tasks, when tackling genuinely difficult problems, domain knowledge matters far more than language features. The most impressive software systems - from operating systems to distributed databases to major web services - are overwhelmingly built in these supposedly boring languages, and the best engineers typically have deep domain expertise rather than expertise in exotic languages.
quotes.key_insight: The value of a programming language for difficult work lies not in its features for reducing boilerplate, but in the quality of engineers and systems it gives you access to - and empirically, the world's most impressive software is built in "boring" languages by people with deep domain expertise.

quotes.quote: I don't think about things in a programming language. I think of an abstract solution and then translate the solution to a language, so having another language in my toolbox can, at most, help me think of better translations and save on translation.

quotes.quote: It is very common for bugs that have complex real-world manifestations to be reproducible with really simple test cases, if you know where to look.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that software testing relies too heavily on manual test generation compared to hardware testing, where automated random and coverage-based testing dominate. He demonstrates that simple random test frameworks (like QuickCheck) and coverage-guided fuzzers (like AFL) can find the vast majority of production bugs with minimal effort, yet software projects underutilize these methods due to cultural path dependence rather than rational cost-benefit analysis. The hardware industry's assumption that all critical bugs must be caught before release drives investment in sophisticated automated testing, while software's acceptance of bugs escaping to production perpetuates inefficient manual testing practices.
quotes.key_insight: The software industry's reliance on manual testing over automated random and coverage-based approaches is a cultural artifact rather than a rational choice, leaving easily-findable bugs in production systems despite cheap, proven alternatives.

quotes.quote: You can spend an hour implementing a random test generator and find tens of bugs, or you can spend more time and find thousands of bugs.

quotes.quote: If you have very narrow optimization goals, and your measurements don't give you any visibility into anything else, everything but your optimization goals is going to get thrown out the window.
quotes.summary: This post examines how optimizing for intermediate metrics instead of true goals leads to ineffective or counterproductive outcomes across domains. Using three case studies—IQ scores in early childhood education, cholesterol levels in heart disease prevention, and percentile latency in software systems—Luu demonstrates that when proxy metrics become targets, they can be improved without (or even at the expense of) the actual desired outcome. The fundamental problem is Goodhart's Law in action: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
quotes.key_insight: Optimizing intermediate proxy metrics without validating their continued connection to ultimate goals is a universal failure mode across domains—from education to medicine to software performance—requiring constant vigilance to ensure the map matches the territory.

quotes.quote: It's like the video game solving AI that presses pause before its character is about to get killed, because pausing the game prevents its health from decreasing.

quotes.quote: I've been hearing this question a lot lately, and when I do, it reminds me how much I don't know.
quotes.summary: This post catalogs the enormous depth of technical knowledge gaps exposed by the deceptively simple question 'what happens when you load a URL?' Through 48 progressively deeper questions spanning keyboards, networks, fiber optics, browsers, and displays, Luu demonstrates how much complexity lies beneath everyday computing tasks. Rather than providing answers, the post serves as a humbling inventory of how little even experienced engineers truly understand about the full stack. The post concludes with curated resources for learning about these topics and a note that this open-ended exploration represents the opposite of effective technical interviews.
quotes.key_insight: The staggering complexity hidden beneath simple computing tasks reveals how specialized modern engineering has become - no single person can deeply understand the full stack from keyboard physics to fiber optics to browser rendering.

quotes.quote: You have this connection that can handle 10Gb/s. That surely won't work if there's any gap at all between the physical doodads that are being connected. How do people design connectors that can withstand tens of thousands of insertions and still maintain their tolerances?

quotes.quote: It's really common to see claims that some meme is backed by 'studies' or 'science'. But when I look at the actual studies, it usually turns out that the data are opposed to the claim.
quotes.summary: The post systematically debunks several widely-cited scientific findings by actually reading the original papers, showing that popular interpretations of studies on Dunning-Kruger effect, income and happiness, hedonic adaptation, and chess expertise are often opposite to what the research actually found. Luu argues that reading academic papers is much easier than people think - often requiring just reading the abstract, conclusion, and skimming graphs to verify claims. The post emphasizes that gatekeeping around scientific literacy is often self-serving, as the knowledge required to interpret most studies can be acquired in far less time than a full degree program.
quotes.key_insight: Most popular interpretations of scientific studies are wrong because people don't actually read the papers, but verifying claims by reading the original research is far easier than the gatekeepers of scientific literacy would have you believe.

quotes.quote: I think this misses two very important factors. One, is that jobs that are on the potential chopping block, such as first-line customer service, customer service for industries that are either low margin or don't care about the customer, etc., tend to be filled by apathetic humans in a poorly designed system, and humans aren't even very good at simple tasks they care a lot about.
quotes.summary: The common debate over AI replacing humans focuses on whether AI can match ideal human performance, but this misses two key points: many customer service jobs are already filled by apathetic humans in poorly-designed systems who perform terribly, and companies will replace humans with AI even if AI is significantly worse, as long as it's much cheaper. Through detailed examples of frustrating bureaucratic interactions with healthcare scheduling and auto service, the post demonstrates that we're already at the point where computers can match apathetic humans in poorly-designed systems, which describes a significant fraction of service jobs. The 2023 update confirms this prediction, noting that LLM-based customer service is indeed replacing human workers despite being imperfect, because human customer service wasn't improving while AI continues to get better.
quotes.key_insight: AI doesn't need to match the performance of ideal humans to displace workers—it only needs to match the performance of apathetic humans in broken systems while being dramatically cheaper, a bar that has already been cleared in many industries.

quotes.quote: I'm not going to claim that CPU backdoors exist, but I will claim that the implementation is easy, if you've got the right access.
quotes.summary: While backdoors in software are well-documented, the possibility of CPU-level hardware backdoors remains dismissed as impossible despite being technically straightforward to implement. The post details three components of a CPU backdoor: what it would do (privilege escalation from user to kernel mode), how it would be triggered (through carefully chosen instruction sequences with specific input values), and how it would be inserted (either through compromising the design process, manufacturing, or the microcode patch mechanism that all modern CPUs use for bug fixes). The technical barriers are minimal compared to the organizational challenges of gaining the necessary access.
quotes.key_insight: CPU backdoors are not only technically feasible but relatively simple to implement through existing patch mechanisms and manufacturing processes—the real barrier is organizational access, not technical difficulty.

quotes.quote: A few cycles vs. 400+ cycles sounds really bad; that's well over 100x slower. But if I write a dumb loop that reads and operates on a large block of 64-bit values, the CPU is smart enough to prefetch the correct data before I need it, which lets me process at about 22 GB/s on my 3GHz processor.
quotes.summary: Modern CPUs have evolved far beyond simple arithmetic and logic operations, adding complex memory hierarchies with caches and TLBs, out-of-order execution, SIMD instructions, and sophisticated concurrency primitives. While many optimizations like branch prediction and cache management happen transparently, programmers can achieve significant performance gains by understanding memory access patterns, cache locality, and avoiding expensive syscalls. The gap between memory and CPU speed has grown to 100x+, making memory hierarchy understanding critical, though clever prefetching and caching often mask this latency automatically.
quotes.key_insight: Modern CPU performance is dominated by memory hierarchy effects rather than instruction execution speed, and while many optimizations happen transparently, understanding cache behavior and avoiding syscalls matters far more than traditional micro-optimizations like branch prediction or alignment that CPUs now handle well automatically.

quotes.quote: The real cost of not locking also often ends up being the inevitable bugs. Doing clever things with memory barriers is almost always a bug waiting to happen... The fact is, any time anybody makes up a new locking mechanism, THEY ALWAYS GET IT WRONG.

The gap between memory and CPU speed has grown to 100x+, making memory hierarchy understanding critical, though clever prefetching and caching often mask this latency automatically.

quotes.quote: The wildly optimistic success rate is that you barely cover rent when the programming job market is hot enough that mid-level positions at big companies pay out total compensation that's 8x-9x the median income in the U.S. That's not good.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu analyzes whether running ads on his blog makes financial sense, ultimately concluding it probably doesn't despite potential revenue of $600-2,300/month. He examines the tradeoffs between modest ad revenue (which would never approach his tech salary) versus downsides like reduced traffic, privacy concerns, perverse incentives, and hassle. Through updates spanning two years, he discovers his traffic grew enough to theoretically support living expenses, yet still chooses not to run ads due to the hassle factor, comparing this to developers who rationally leave money on the table by choosing startups over higher-paying big tech jobs.
quotes.key_insight: Even when blog monetization becomes theoretically lucrative enough to support living expenses, the rational choice for someone with tech industry earning power may be to leave that money on the table if the hassle and tradeoffs don't align with personal preferences - just as developers rationally choose lower-paying jobs they prefer.

quotes.quote: Hiring women . . . gave Townsend-Greenspan higher-quality work for the same money
quotes.summary: This post systematically dismantles the argument that market forces prevent discrimination in tech hiring. Using evidence from sports (baseball, basketball, hockey), finance (Alan Greenspan's economics firm), and empirical studies, Luu demonstrates that even in highly competitive markets with measurable performance metrics, discrimination persists for decades and actually creates competitive advantages for non-discriminating firms. The post argues that claims about 'lowering the bar' through diversity efforts are unfounded because current hiring practices are far from optimal, and that 'the market' is just collective human action that won't self-correct without intentional intervention.
quotes.key_insight: The appeal to 'market forces' as a defense against claims of tech industry discrimination is empirically false - even in more competitive markets with better performance metrics like professional sports, discrimination persisted for decades and created profit opportunities for non-discriminating firms, proving that markets alone don't eliminate bias without deliberate intervention.

quotes.quote: In tech, some people are concerned that increasing diversity will 'lower the bar', but in sports, which has a more competitive hiring market than tech, we saw the opposite, increasing diversity raised the level instead of lowering it because it means hiring people on their qualifications instead of on what they look like.

quotes.quote: A common trick to work around this is to store meta-information about a memory region in some space that we squirrel away just below the pointer that we return.
quotes.summary: This tutorial walks through implementing a simple malloc/free memory allocator from scratch using sbrk to manipulate the heap. The implementation uses a linked list of memory blocks with metadata stored just below returned pointers, supports realloc and calloc, and can be dynamically linked to replace the system allocator in existing binaries. The post includes detailed debugging techniques using gdb and exercises to improve the basic implementation with proper alignment, block splitting, and block merging.
quotes.key_insight: Memory allocators work by requesting contiguous heap space from the OS and managing it through metadata hidden just before returned pointers, creating a reusable pool of blocks that can't be returned to the OS piecemeal but must be tracked and recycled within the application's address space.

quotes.quote: I spent half an hour writing a throwaway script and ran into four bugs in the core language.
quotes.summary: Luu provides a detailed critique of Julia's engineering culture and practices, arguing that despite the language's impressive technical features and performance claims, it suffers from systematic quality issues stemming from inadequate testing, poor error handling, and a core team culture that dismisses legitimate concerns. The post catalogs numerous bugs encountered in basic usage, examines structural problems in the development process (lack of code review, poor documentation, weak test coverage), and critiques misleading performance benchmarks. A 2022 update demonstrates that many core issues persist years later despite claims they were fixed, with the community's reflexive defensiveness and PR-focused responses preventing meaningful improvement in correctness and reliability.
quotes.key_insight: Julia's core problem isn't technical limitations but a development culture that systematically undervalues correctness, testing, and documentation while publicly claiming excellence in these areas, creating a gap between marketed reliability and actual user experience that persists because legitimate criticism is reflexively dismissed rather than addressed.

quotes.quote: That's awful (although not atypical) compiler generated code... Had the compiler just done the add without the additional mov, leaving the input in %edi/%esi as it was passed (and as done in the non-checked version), this wouldn't be necessary.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu analyzes the actual performance cost of integer overflow checking in C/C++ compilers, finding that the theoretical overhead should be around 2-5% for typical workloads based on instruction mix analysis. However, practical benchmarks reveal that current compiler implementations (clang and gcc circa 2014) produce dramatically worse code when overflow diagnostics are enabled - not due to the overflow checks themselves, but because of poor register allocation and mis-optimizations that add unnecessary pushes, pops, and moves. When using trap-on-error mode instead of diagnostic mode, the performance penalty essentially disappears, demonstrating that the problem is fixable compiler implementation rather than fundamental hardware limitations.
quotes.key_insight: The performance penalty of integer overflow checking is a solved hardware problem (overflow flags exist), but poor compiler implementation creates artificial 4-6x slowdowns that disappear when diagnostics are disabled, revealing that the barrier to adoption is compiler quality rather than fundamental limitations.

quotes.quote: While a better C compiler could do better, in theory, gcc 4.82 doesn't do better than clang 3.4 here. For one thing, gcc's -ftrapv only checks signed overflow. Worse yet, it doesn't work, and this bug on ftrapv has been open since 2008.

quotes.quote: testing is hopelessly inadequate....(it) can be used very effectively to show the presence of bugs but never to show their absence.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu challenges the common claim that 'informal reasoning' is superior to testing for ensuring software correctness, particularly critiquing arguments from 'Out of the Tar Pit.' He argues that many real-world systems—from modern CPUs to tax code implementations—contain irreducible complexity that cannot be reasoned about formally, and that humans inevitably make reasoning errors. Drawing from 7.5 years in CPU verification, he demonstrates that comprehensive testing can achieve extremely high reliability (one logical bug in 7.5 years), making it not just necessary but practically sufficient for most engineering purposes.
quotes.key_insight: While testing cannot prove the absence of bugs theoretically, practical evidence shows it can achieve reliability levels that exceed what most organizations require, making the 'informal reasoning over testing' argument actively harmful for real-world engineering.

quotes.quote: How is it possible that someone with zero knowledge of web development can get that kind of speedup by watching one presentation and then futzing around for 25 minutes?
quotes.summary: Dan Luu optimizes his Octopress static blog hosted on GitHub Pages, achieving a 25-50x speedup by removing unnecessary JavaScript, fonts, and CSS. Starting with a 12-second load time, he systematically eliminates bloat like unused modernizr/jQuery dependencies, custom fonts, and unminified CSS to reach 400ms. The post demonstrates how someone with zero web development knowledge can achieve GPU-to-ASIC-level performance gains simply by removing the default cruft that comes with popular frameworks, raising questions about why widely-used platforms ship with such massive performance problems.
quotes.key_insight: Popular web frameworks and platforms ship with such egregious performance problems that removing all their default features can yield hardware-optimization-level speedups, suggesting the industry has normalized absurdly slow websites.

quotes.quote: Having no styling is surely not optimal, but there's something a bit funny about no styling beating the at-the-time most widely used programmer blog styling, which means it likely also beat wordpress, svtble, blogspot, medium, etc.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu optimizes his Octopress static blog hosted on GitHub Pages, achieving a 25-50x speedup by removing unnecessary JavaScript, fonts, and CSS. Starting with a 12-second load time, he systematically eliminates bloat like unused modernizr/jQuery dependencies, custom fonts, and unminified CSS to reach 400ms. The post demonstrates how someone with zero web development knowledge can achieve GPU-to-ASIC-level performance gains simply by removing the default cruft that comes with popular frameworks, raising questions about why widely-used platforms ship with such massive performance problems.
quotes.key_insight: Popular web frameworks and platforms ship with such egregious performance problems that removing all their default features can yield hardware-optimization-level speedups, suggesting the industry has normalized absurdly slow websites.

quotes.quote: Alright, so their most frequently used words are to, alsa, the, and the. Turns out, Takashi Iwai (tiwai) often works on audio (alsa), and by going down the list we can see that David Miller's (davem) fifth most frequently used term is sparc64, which is a pretty good indicator that he does a lot of sparc work. But the table is mostly noise.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu applies TF-IDF (term frequency-inverse document frequency) analysis to Linux kernel commit messages from 2005 onwards to identify what different contributors work on. Simple word frequency counts are dominated by common words like 'the' and 'to', and manually filtering stop words still leaves too much noise. By applying TF-IDF to weight terms by how distinctive they are to each contributor, he successfully reveals clear patterns: for example, Takashi Iwai works on ALSA audio codecs, David Miller on SPARC64, and Ralf Baechle on MIPS.
quotes.key_insight: TF-IDF provides a simple, effective technique for identifying what distinguishes individual contributors in large collaborative projects by weighting terms by their uniqueness rather than just their frequency.

quotes.quote: I actually don't understand why it's standard to take the log here. Sometimes you want to take the log so you can work with smaller numbers, or so that you can convert a bunch of multiplies into a bunch of adds, but neither of those is true here.

quotes.quote: TF-IDF is sometimes useful and incredibly easy to apply. You should use it when you should use it (when you want to see what words distinguish different documents/people from each other) and you shouldn't use it when you shouldn't use it (when you want to see what's common to documents/people).

quotes.quote: If I had to guess, I'd say I probably work around hundreds of bugs in an average week, and thousands in a bad week.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu documents encountering approximately 80 new bugs in a single week across various software platforms (Ubuntu, GitHub, Julia, Google products, games, etc.) to demonstrate how pervasive software quality issues are. He argues that while everyone knows they should write more tests, the conventional approach of manual unit testing is too labor-intensive to be practical, especially given that complexity doesn't scale linearly with code size. Instead, he advocates for property-based testing, fuzzing, and other automated testing approaches that can find dozens of bugs in hours rather than requiring years of manual test-writing effort.
quotes.key_insight: Since developers won't invest the massive effort required for comprehensive manual testing, the path forward is automated testing approaches like fuzzing that can find orders of magnitude more bugs per hour of developer time invested.

quotes.quote: I think programmers who doubt that type systems help are basically the tech equivalent of an anti-vaxxer.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu systematically reviews empirical studies on static versus dynamic typing, finding that most controlled experiments show very small effects, if any. The studies suffer from serious methodological flaws including trivial toy tasks, student populations, language familiarity bias, and flawed data classification. While some researchers make strong claims about type systems improving code quality, the empirical evidence is weak, limited in scope, and often contradictory, with programmer variance typically dwarfing any language effects.
quotes.key_insight: Despite strong ideological claims on both sides of the static-versus-dynamic typing debate, the actual empirical evidence is weak, methodologically flawed, and shows effects that are typically smaller than individual programmer variation—yet researchers and practitioners continue to cite these studies as definitive proof of their preferred position.

quotes.quote: next generation storage technologies (PCM, 3d XPoint, etc.), will be fast enough that syscall and other OS overhead can be more expensive than the actual cost of the disk access
quotes.summary: Intel's new CLWB and PCOMMIT instructions address performance bottlenecks that emerge when non-volatile RAM becomes fast enough that OS overhead and cache hierarchy impedance dominate transaction costs. The post explains how next-generation storage (PCM, 3D XPoint) creates an Amdahl's law problem where traditional memory caching policies (writeback, writethrough, etc.) impose orders-of-magnitude slowdowns when persistence guarantees are needed. CLWB allows cache writeback without invalidation, while PCOMMIT ensures stores are committed to persistent storage, potentially solving the impedance mismatch between x86's cache hierarchy and NVRAM without requiring cache flushes everywhere.
quotes.key_insight: Fast NVRAM creates an Amdahl's law bottleneck where cache coherency protocols and OS overhead become the dominant cost, requiring new CPU instructions like CLWB and PCOMMIT to maintain performance while guaranteeing persistence.

quotes.quote: Web programmers are hyper-aware of how 100ms of extra latency on a web page load has a noticeable effect on conversion rate. Well, what's the effect on conversion rate when a potential contributor to your project spends 20 minutes installing dependencies and an hour building your project only to find the build is broken?
quotes.summary: Dan Luu analyzes Travis CI data from 40 popular GitHub projects and finds that most open source projects have surprisingly unreliable builds compared to corporate environments. While 99% uptime (two 9s) would be considered poor in professional settings, only 8 of 40 open source projects achieve this baseline, with 15 projects failing to even reach 90% uptime. This is despite the fact that keeping builds working is technically trivial (the "not rocket science rule") and broken builds waste contributor time just as surely as they waste employee dollars.
quotes.key_insight: Open source projects systematically tolerate levels of build unreliability that would be unacceptable in corporate environments, wasting contributor time despite having trivial technical solutions available, because they don't translate the cost of broken builds into wasted human effort the way businesses translate it to wasted money.

quotes.quote: Graydon Hoare literally calls keeping a clean build the 'not rocket science rule', and wrote an open source tool (bors) anyone can use to do not-rocket-science. And yet, most open source projects still suffer through broken and failed builds.

quotes.quote: If you have a tight loop, LRU is going to be perfect as long as the loop fits in cache, but it's going to cause a miss every time if the loop doesn't fit. A random eviction policy degrades gracefully as the loop gets too big.
quotes.summary: This post empirically tests cache eviction policies, particularly comparing LRU (least recently used) to a randomized approach of choosing the LRU between two random cache entries (2-random). Through extensive SPEC CPU benchmark simulations across different cache sizes and hierarchies, Luu demonstrates that while LRU performs better for smaller caches, 2-random actually outperforms LRU for larger caches (especially above 1MB). The counterintuitive finding is rooted in mathematical probability theory about load balancing—choosing between k random options dramatically improves performance with diminishing returns after k=2, making 2-random nearly as effective as full LRU while being cheaper to implement.
quotes.key_insight: Counterintuitively, making cache eviction decisions based on just two random samples performs nearly as well as tracking full LRU state, and actually beats LRU for large caches—a practical application of the 'power of two choices' theorem showing that a small amount of randomized choice can match expensive deterministic algorithms.

quotes.quote: The mathematical intuition is that if we (randomly) throw n balls into n bins, the maximum number of balls in any bin is O(log n / log log n) with high probability, which is pretty much just O(log n). But if (instead of choosing randomly) we choose the least loaded of k random bins, the maximum is O(log log n / log k) with high probability.
quotes.summary: This post empirically tests cache eviction policies, particularly comparing LRU (least recently used) to a randomized approach of choosing the LRU between two random cache entries (2-random). quotes.quote: Random and FIFO are both strictly worse than either LRU or 2-random. LRU and 2-random are pretty close, with LRU edging out 2-random for the smaller caches and 2-random edging out LRU for the larger caches.

quotes.quote: The problem is that intrinsics are so unreliable that you have to manually check the result on every platform and every compiler you expect your code to be run on, and then tweak the intrinsics until you get a reasonable result. That's more work than just writing the assembly by hand.
quotes.summary: The post demonstrates that intrinsics, despite promises of being a portable and compiler-optimized alternative to hand-written assembly, remain unreliable and often produce suboptimal code. Through a detailed case study of the `popcnt` (population count) instruction, the author shows how naive use of intrinsics can be 3x slower than hand-written assembly due to compiler failures like not unrolling loops, generating backwards accumulation patterns, and not working around known CPU bugs. The analysis reveals that achieving optimal performance still requires manually checking assembly output across compilers and platforms, making intrinsics more work than just writing assembly directly.
quotes.key_insight: Intrinsics promise compiler-optimized portable performance but in practice require the same manual verification and platform-specific tweaking as assembly, making them more work rather than less for high-performance code.

quotes.quote: Unexpected distributions in analytics are a sign of a misunderstanding, which is often a sign of a bug.
quotes.summary: Software companies should use the analytics data they already collect to automatically detect bugs, rather than relying solely on traditional testing. By analyzing user behavior patterns—like conversion rates through UI flows, unusual action sequences, or actions followed by disconnections—teams can identify bugs that users encounter in production. This approach mirrors how hardware engineers use performance counters to detect design flaws, and could catch a significant portion of bugs with minimal additional effort since the data is already being collected for other purposes.
quotes.key_insight: Companies obsessively optimize conversion funnels for revenue but ignore that the same analytics infrastructure could automatically detect the bugs that destroy conversion rates far more dramatically than any UI tweak could improve them.

quotes.quote: But in software land, I'm lucky if a random project I want to contribute to has tests at all.
quotes.summary: Software companies should use the analytics data they already collect to automatically detect bugs, rather than relying solely on traditional testing. By analyzing user behavior patterns—like conversion rates through UI flows, unusual action sequences, or actions followed by disconnections—teams can identify bugs that users encounter in production. This approach mirrors how hardware engineers use performance counters to detect design flaws, and could catch a significant portion of bugs with minimal additional effort since the data is already being collected for other purposes.
quotes.key_insight: Companies obsessively optimize conversion funnels for revenue but ignore that the same analytics infrastructure could automatically detect the bugs that destroy conversion rates far more dramatically than any UI tweak could improve them.

quotes.quote: The claims are awfully strong, considering that the main study cited only looked at people who graduated with a B.S. exactly one year ago, not to mention the fact that the study makes literally the opposite claim.
quotes.summary: Dan Luu systematically debunks a viral Quartz article claiming there's no gender pay gap in tech by carefully analyzing the very sources it cites. He demonstrates that the AAUW study cited actually shows women earning 77-88% of what men earn in tech fields, with one-third of the gap unexplainable by any factor except gender. The Quartz article selectively cherry-picked data, misrepresented findings, and ignored contradictory evidence from its own sources to support a predetermined conclusion.
quotes.key_insight: Viral tech journalism often works backwards from a desired conclusion, selectively cherry-picking and misrepresenting data from authoritative sources to support predetermined narratives, even when those sources explicitly contradict the article's claims.

quotes.quote: If you have the first view, breaking out a hex editor to modify a program is the action of a deranged lunatic. If you have the second view, editing binaries is the most natural thing in the world.
quotes.summary: Binary editing is a practical technique for patching compiled programs when source code isn't available or recompiling isn't feasible. The author argues that while this seems shocking to developers who think exclusively in terms of source code, it's natural for those who understand that processors simply execute bits. Through concrete examples - from fixing bugs in closed-source software to handling emergency deployment situations - Luu demonstrates that binary editing is often the quickest path to solving real-world problems.
quotes.key_insight: Understanding programs as processor instructions rather than just source code transformations unlocks powerful problem-solving capabilities for emergencies and workarounds that would otherwise require lengthy recompilation processes.

quotes.quote: Larry Ellison wasn't happy with the results and it's said that he tried to have DeWitt fired.
quotes.summary: In 1983, University of Wisconsin researchers published database benchmarks showing Oracle performed dramatically worse than competitors—sometimes 30-100x slower. Oracle's Larry Ellison reportedly tried to have the lead researcher fired, then banned benchmarking in Oracle's EULA. This spawned "DeWitt Clauses" that spread across the software industry, preventing customers from publicly comparing performance. The post argues this anti-competitive practice harms transparency and suggests creating anonymous benchmark markets to circumvent these restrictions.
quotes.key_insight: When vendors prohibit benchmarking in their licenses, it's effectively an admission that their performance is so poor that public comparison would be more damaging than the reputational cost of appearing afraid of transparency.

quotes.quote: Why do we leave material out of classes and then fail students who can't figure out that material for themselves?
quotes.summary: Luu argues that engineering education systematically fails students by refusing to teach fundamental debugging skills, instead treating high failure rates as a badge of honor that weeds out those who "can't hack it." He recounts discovering systematic debugging through necessity during a divider project, realizing that many struggling students weren't lacking intelligence but simply had never been taught methodical problem-solving approaches. The education system's pride in its "leaky STEM pipeline" is a manufactured crisis—schools could easily teach these skills but choose not to, preferring hazing rituals over actual education.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering education's high failure rates are a manufactured crisis resulting from deliberate choices not to teach fundamental debugging skills, not evidence that struggling students lack aptitude.

quotes.quote: I've often regretted not taking a class seriously because I've later found myself in a situation where I could have put the skills to good use, I've never said to myself 'what a waste of time it was to learn that fundamental mathematical concept and use it enough to that I truly understand it.'
quotes.summary: The author examines whether programmers actually need math by reviewing their nine undergraduate math courses and finding that seven of nine have proven directly useful in non-math-oriented programming work. While acknowledging some selection bias (math-heavy companies are more likely to hire them), they conclude that math has provided valuable tools even in low-level systems work through combinatorial proofs, numerical approximations, and algorithmic insights. The recommendation is nuanced: math is worthwhile if you enjoy it and it may bias your career toward interesting mathematical work, but it's not the most efficient path if your sole goal is transitioning from academia to programming.
quotes.key_insight: Math knowledge proves surprisingly useful across programming domains, not just in obviously mathematical work, though its value depends on whether you genuinely enjoy it rather than viewing it purely as career preparation.

quotes.quote: If this benchmark seems contrived, it actually comes from a real world example of the disastrous performance implications of using nice power of 2 alignment, or page alignment in an actual system
quotes.summary: This post explains how cache line alignment can dramatically hurt performance through conflict misses. When data is page-aligned (or aligned to large powers of 2), addresses map to the same cache sets, artificially limiting cache capacity - for example, reducing a 32kB L1 cache to effectively only 8 useful locations. By misaligning data across different cache lines, the same hardware can utilize 512 locations in L1, yielding substantial performance improvements. The post uses empirical benchmarks and detailed cache organization analysis to demonstrate this counterintuitive result: 'nice' alignment can be disastrous.
quotes.key_insight: Counterintuitively, 'clean' power-of-2 memory alignment can cripple performance by forcing all accesses into the same cache sets, reducing effective cache capacity by orders of magnitude compared to deliberately misaligned data.

quotes.quote: If we use the cache optimally, we can store 32,768 items. But, since we're accessing things that are page (4k) aligned, we effectively lose the bottom log₂(4k) = 12 bits, which means that every access falls into the same set, and we can only loop through 8 things before our working set is too large to fit in the L1!
quotes.summary: This post explains how cache line alignment can dramatically hurt performance through conflict misses. When data is page-aligned (or aligned to large powers of 2), addresses map to the same cache sets, artificially limiting cache capacity - for example, reducing a 32kB L1 cache to effectively only 8 useful locations. quotes.key_insight: Counterintuitively, 'clean' power-of-2 memory alignment can cripple performance by forcing all accesses into the same cache sets, reducing effective cache capacity by orders of magnitude compared to deliberately misaligned data.

quotes.quote: Not only will aligning to any large power of 2 cause a problem, generating addresses with a power of 2 offset from each other will cause the same problem

quotes.quote: Nowadays, the importance of caches is well understood enough that, when I'm asked to look at a cache related performance bug, it's usually due to the kind of thing we just talked about: conflict misses that prevent us from using our full cache effectively

quotes.quote: When all you have are linear hammers, non-linear screws look a lot like nails.
quotes.summary: Luu recounts an interview where he disagreed with an interviewer who believed PCA (Principal Component Analysis) was a universal solution for all classification problems. Using empirical examples comparing LSA (a linear dimensionality reduction technique) to deep learning autoencoders for text classification, he demonstrates that linear methods have fundamental limitations when dealing with nuanced, complex patterns. The post critiques the broader trend of data scientists mechanically applying a small set of linear techniques to every problem, regardless of whether those methods are appropriate for the problem's inherent structure.
quotes.key_insight: The popularity of data science has led many practitioners to mechanically apply simple linear techniques to complex problems that fundamentally require non-linear approaches, producing poor results with misplaced confidence.

quotes.quote: Every single such startup I know of failed
quotes.summary: Hardware development, particularly CPU design, requires decades of accumulated experience and cannot be successfully executed by inexperienced teams, no matter how smart. Unlike software startups where young founders often succeed, every CPU startup that relied heavily on inexperienced engineers has failed, burning through hundreds of millions in funding. Physical work involves tacit knowledge that cannot be derived from first principles or learned from books alone, as illustrated by South Korea's multi-decade journey to build a shipbuilding industry despite access to expert consultants and detailed plans.
quotes.key_insight: The unforgiving nature of physical mistakes in hardware development makes experience indispensable, as there is no undo button and fundamental errors cannot be iteratively fixed like in software.

quotes.quote: It's non-optimal that an article that will receive 50 votes in expectation has a 60% chance of getting 100+ votes, and a 40% chance of getting 2 votes.
quotes.summary: Social news sites like HN and Reddit have a fundamental threshold problem where articles that don't get early votes never reach the front page, while those that do get disproportionate exposure. This creates inefficiency where content quality doesn't correlate well with visibility - success depends heavily on random early voters. The proposed solution is counterintuitive: add random noise to article rankings to smooth out the discontinuity between front-page success and obscurity, similar to probabilistic counting algorithms in computer science.
quotes.key_insight: Social news aggregation suffers from a threshold effect where early randomness has outsized impact on visibility, and while adding algorithmic randomness seems counterintuitive, human curation ultimately proves more effective than pure algorithmic solutions.

quotes.quote: By adding a small amount of random noise to the rank of an article, we can smooth out the discontinuity between making it onto the front page and languishing in obscurity.

quotes.quote: This behavior, eminently reasonable on the part of any individual, results in a systemic failure, a tax on new open source contributors.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that open source projects with large backlogs of ignored pull requests create a systemic barrier to new contributors, even when individual maintainer behavior is reasonable. He describes abandoning contributions to a project after seeing dozens of good patches languishing ignored on the mailing list. The post calls on maintainers with commit access to take ownership of the pull request process or stop soliciting contributions, emphasizing that response time matters more than perfection.
quotes.key_insight: Open source maintainers must either actively manage their pull request queue or stop soliciting contributions—ignoring contributions creates a systemic barrier to participation that discourages future contributors regardless of how reasonable individual maintainers' constraints are.

quotes.quote: As a software engineer, how would you feel if 99% of valid Java code ended up being translated to something that produced random results, even though tests pass on the untranslated Java code?
quotes.summary: Verilog, the dominant hardware design language in the US, was originally designed as a simulation language and is fundamentally ill-suited for describing actual hardware. This mismatch means that the vast majority of syntactically valid Verilog code produces either nonsensical or unsynthesizable results, requiring engineers to mentally picture hardware first and then awkwardly translate it into Verilog's C-like constructs. Despite numerous attempts at better alternatives (Haskell-based languages like Lava and Bluespec, or Scala-based Chisel), adoption has been stymied by politics and industry conservatism rather than technical merit.
quotes.key_insight: Verilog's fundamental design as a simulation language rather than a hardware description language creates a massive impedance mismatch that forces engineers to work around the language rather than with it, yet better alternatives fail to gain adoption due to political and cultural resistance rather than technical deficiencies.

quotes.quote: We hardware folks are so used to the vast majority of legal Verilog constructs producing unsynthesizable garbage that we don't find it the least bit surprising that you not only do you not get compile-time errors, you don't even get run-time errors, from writing naive Verilog code.

quotes.quote: To write Verilog that will produce correct hardware, you have to first picture the hardware you want to produce. Then, you have to figure out how to describe that in this weird C-like simulation language.

quotes.quote: Accessing a signal in a different clock domain without synchronization is like accessing a data structure from multiple threads without synchronization. Sort of. But worse. Much worse.
quotes.summary: Luu argues that Verilog development desperately needs basic static analysis tools to catch common bugs early, despite the industry's lack of such tooling. He describes how a simple custom linter based on naming conventions (for pipeline stages, clock domains, and module boundaries) found 7 bugs in 30 minutes, demonstrating that you don't need a PhD to build useful static analysis for hardware design. The post challenges the notion that static analysis is too hard, showing that simple checks can prevent costly errors that take hours to debug in simulation or days to discover in timing reports.
quotes.key_insight: The hardware industry lacks basic static analysis tooling that software engineers take for granted, but simple custom linters can find critical bugs in minutes that would otherwise take days to discover through simulation or synthesis.
